context: there's nothing that can separate me from God's love
point_summary: Nothing can separate believers from God's love, and it is precisely that unbreakable love which motivates his rebuking and chastening of the Laodicean church.

reference_text: nothing that can separate us from the love of God
context: By the way, aren't you thankful that there's nothing that can separate us from the love of God?
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: ...the gospel is the power of God unto salvation...
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: we are more than conquerors through Christ
point_summary: Victory over false teaching and the world belongs to believers not through their own strength, but because greater is He that is in them—a truth echoed in the "more than conquerors" language of Romans 8:37.

context: The Bible says this in Romans 5 and verse number 8, but God commendeth His love. Again, we see those words, toward us, and that while we're yet sinners, Christ died for us.
point_summary: Romans 5:8 is cited to establish that believers are recipients of a love they did not initiate, a love demonstrated concretely in Christ dying for sinners while they were yet sinners.

context: Notice how His love is described for us in the book of Romans, in the eighth chapter, in Romans chapter number 8.
point_summary: Romans 8 is introduced as scriptural testimony to the love of God received by undeserving sinners—a love demonstrated by Christ dying for those who could not save themselves.

context: V. 37 of Romans 8, 'Nay, in all these things we are more than conquerors. Notice this, through Him that loved us.'
point_summary: Victory over all circumstances and opposition is grounded not in human effort but in the love of Christ—"through Him that loved us"—making believers "more than conquerors."

context: 'That neither death, nor life, nor angels, nor principalities, nor powers, nor things present, nor things to come, nor height, nor depth, nor any other creature...'
point_summary: Nothing in all creation—death, life, angels, powers, height, depth—can separate believers from the love of God in Christ Jesus, a love so comprehensive it defies full human comprehension.

context: 'That neither death, nor life, nor angels, nor principalities, nor powers, nor things present, nor things to come, nor height, nor depth, nor any other creature...'
point_summary: Nothing in all creation—death, life, angels, powers, height, depth—can separate believers from the love of God in Christ Jesus, a love so vast it surpasses full human comprehension.

context: the gift of God... the gift of eternal life
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: there's none righteous, no not one
point_summary: Romans 3:10's declaration that none is righteous grounds the closing prayer's assertion that no one can earn salvation through personal goodness or moral effort.

context: Remember the truth of Romans 828. Oftentimes we don't quote the beginning when it says, and we know that all things work together for good to them who love God, to them who are the
point_summary: Everything that happens passes through God's filter for a purpose, and Romans 8:28 grounds that confidence by assuring believers—those who love God and are called according to His purpose—that all things work together for their good and His glory.

context: In Romans chapter 12 beginning in verse number 3, the Bible says, for I say through the grace given unto me to every man that is among you, not to think of himself more highly than he ought to think, but to think soberly according as God hath dealt to every man the measure of faith.
point_summary: Humility about one's role is grounded in recognizing that God sovereignly distributes a distinct measure of faith and a distinct office to each believer, equipping each for his individual calling.

context: so we being many are one body in Christ and every one members one of another
point_summary: Unity in the body of Christ is the framework through which God distributes differing gifts to each member individually, equipping every believer according to his sovereign plan.

context: I want you to notice in Romans chapter number nine and look at this passage of scripture and we'll finish here with this aspect of the symbolism
point_summary: Romans 9's citation of "the elder shall serve the younger" is introduced as a passage many misread through a Calvinist lens, which the sermon explicitly rejects as unbiblical.

reference_text: Romans chapter number nine in verses 10 through 13
context: In Romans chapter number nine in verses 10 through 13, by the way, let me say this, many people get all sorts of afraid and sideways reading Romans chapter number nine
point_summary: Romans 9:10–13 is introduced as the New Testament's return to the Genesis 25 prophecy—"the elder shall serve the younger"—with the preacher signaling that its meaning must be read apart from Calvinist systematic theology.

context: whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved
point_summary: Romans 10:13's "whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved" is marshaled against a Calvinist reading of Romans 9, affirming that God's election of Jacob over Esau concerns national destiny, not individual predestination to hell.

context: And not only this, but when Rebecca also had conceived by one, even by our father Isaac
point_summary: Romans 9:10–12 is read here not as a text about individual election to salvation or damnation, but as God's sovereign choice of a nation—Israel—and the Messianic line through Jacob.

context: For the children being not yet born, neither having done any good or evil, that the purpose of God according to election might stand, not of works, but of him that calleth
point_summary: Romans 9:11's reference to election, the sermon argues, concerns God's choice of a national and messianic line through Jacob, not the predestination of individuals to salvation or damnation.

context: It was said unto her, The elder shall serve the younger
point_summary: Romans 9:12, in this sermon's reading, concerns God's election of a nation and a messianic line — not the predestination of individuals to salvation or damnation.

context: As it is written, Jacob have I loved, but Esau have I hated
point_summary: Romans 9:13's "Jacob have I loved, but Esau have I hated" refers to God's selection of a nation and a Messianic line, not the predestination of individuals to salvation or damnation.

context: nothing to do with salvation, everything to do with choosing a nation and dealing with the Messianic line.
point_summary: Romans 9:10–13 is interpreted here as concerning God's selection of a nation and the Messianic line, explicitly not individual election to salvation.

context: And I want you to look at Romans 6, and we're going to look at several verses here, and I hope this will be a blessing to you this evening as we consider the symbolism that is provided.
point_summary: Romans 6 is introduced as the scriptural basis for understanding how the old man (elder) can be made to serve the new man (younger) — a transformation possible only through Jesus Christ.

context: Romans 6 and verse 6, it says this, knowing this, that our old man is crucified with Him, that the body of sin might be destroyed,
point_summary: The co-crucifixion of the old man with Christ is the only basis by which the old nature can be made to serve the new, making subjugation of the flesh possible solely through Jesus.

context: that henceforth we should not serve sin. For he that is dead is freed from sin.
point_summary: The inner conflict between the old man and the new man that believers experience after salvation is resolved not by willpower but by reckoning oneself dead to sin and yielding to Christ.

context: Now if we be dead with Christ, we believe that we shall also live with Him,
point_summary: Death with Christ and resurrection with Him is the ground by which the old nature is made to serve the new, not through willpower but through yielding to Christ.

context: knowing that Christ, being raised from the dead, dieth no more, death hath no more dominion over Him.
point_summary: Christ's resurrection establishes that death has no more dominion over Him, grounding the believer's reckoning of the old man as dead and the new man as alive unto God.

context: For in that he died, he died unto sin once, but in that he liveth, he liveth unto God.
point_summary: Christ's once-for-all death to sin, cited from Romans 6:10, grounds the sermon's point that the old nature's defeat over the new is accomplished through yielding to Christ, not human willpower.

context: Likewise, in like manner, reckon ye also yourselves to be dead indeed unto sin, but alive unto God.
point_summary: The inner struggle between the old and new natures that begins at the new birth is resolved not by willpower but by reckoning oneself dead to sin and yielding to Christ, as Romans 6 commands.

context: Let not sin therefore reign in your mortal body, that you should obey it and the lusts thereof.
point_summary: The inner conflict between the old and new natures, born at regeneration, is resolved not by willpower but by yielding to Christ, as Romans 6 commands believers to reckon themselves dead to sin and alive to God.

context: Neither yield ye your members as instruments of unrighteousness. righteousness unto sin, but yield yourselves unto God, as those that are alive from the dead, and your members as instruments of righteousness unto God.
point_summary: The inner struggle between old and new natures after salvation is resolved not by willpower but by yielding one's members to God as instruments of righteousness through Christ.

context: For sin shall not have dominion over you, for you're not under the law, but under grace.
point_summary: The freedom of the new nature over the old is secured not by human willpower but by grace, as sin's dominion is broken through union with Christ.

context: In Romans chapter number 12, we read of that great truth in verses 1 and 2 where Paul gives this really a culmination of the book of Romans and he comes to this 12th chapter and he says,
point_summary: Presenting one's body as a living sacrifice and renewing the mind are framed as the means of resisting the progressive conformity to the world that leads to spiritual ineffectiveness.

context: In Romans chapter number 12, we read of that great truth in verses 1 and 2 where Paul gives this really a culmination of the book of Romans and he comes to this 12th chapter and he says,
point_summary: Conformity to the world is the danger to be resisted, and transformation through mind-renewal is the God-appointed means of halting the progressive drift into worldliness.

context: I beseech you therefore, brethren, by the mercies of God, that you present your bodies a living sacrifice, holy, acceptable unto God, which is your reasonable service
point_summary: Romans 12:1–2 is cited as Paul's culminating call against worldly conformity, presenting bodily consecration and mental renewal as the believer's defense against the progressive drift toward worldliness.

context: and be not conformed to this world, but be transformed, how? By the renewing of your mind that you may prove what is that good and acceptable and perfect will of God
point_summary: Conformity to the world follows a gradual progression, and Romans 12:2's command to "be not conformed to this world, but be transformed by the renewing of your mind" is presented as the counter to that drift.

context: by the renewing of your mind. Remember what he said as we read that portion of Scripture in Romans 12, in verse number two, he says, be not conformed to this world, but be ye transformed by the renewing of your mind.
point_summary: Avoiding worldly conformity and its pitfalls requires the renewing of the mind, set against the pattern of this world and redirected toward things that are true, honest, just, and pure.

context: Remember at the end of Romans 12 in verse number 2, it says that we might prove what is that good and acceptable and perfect will of God. But we have to be renewed by the transforming of our mind. We're not to be conformed to this world.
point_summary: Conformity to the world is incompatible with proving God's good and perfect will, which requires instead the mind's renewal and transformation.

context: for the wages of sin is death... but the gift of God is eternal life
point_summary: Romans 6:23 is cited to define "the gift of God" from John 4:10 — contrasting the earned wages of sin (death) with the unearned gift of eternal life through Jesus Christ.

context: we understand the Bible tells us in Romans three that there's none that doeth good
point_summary: Romans 3 is cited briefly to establish the universal sinfulness of humanity — that none does good — as the necessary backdrop for understanding why the gift of God in Romans 6:23 is so desperately needed.

context: when the word of God tells us that the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ, our Lord
point_summary: Romans 6:23 is read as Scripture's own definition of "the gift of God" from John 4:10—contrasting the wages of sin (death) with eternal life through Jesus Christ, with the conjunction "but" marking the turn from condemnation to gift.

context: The Bible tells us in Romans 1 and verse number 16, when Paul is declaring this great truth, he says I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ. Why? For it is the power of God unto salvation to everyone that believeth, to the Jew first and also to the Greek.
point_summary: The gospel's universal availability — to every believer, Jew and Greek alike — grounds the claim that God's gift of salvation faces no supply shortage and is accessible to all.

context: We understand that the Word of God teaches us in Romans 10 in verse number 13, for whosoever
point_summary: The universal availability of salvation — "whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord" — is cited to establish that God's gift, unlike scarce earthly goods, is never out of stock for any person.

context: what shall we say then that Abraham, our father, as pertaining to the flesh, hath found?
point_summary: Salvation cannot be earned by works, as Abraham's justification by faith rather than works demonstrates — leaving no room for human boasting before God.

context: For if Abraham were justified by works, he hath whereof to glory, but not before God.
point_summary: Abraham's hypothetical justification by works would have given him grounds to boast before men, but Romans 4:2 is used here to show that salvation by works is excluded precisely because God alone receives the glory.

context: for what saith the Scripture, Abraham believed God, and it was counted unto him for righteousness.
point_summary: Abraham's justification by faith rather than works — counted as righteousness, not earned as wages — demonstrates that salvation is a gift to be received, not a debt God owes.

context: the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord
point_summary: Eternal life, freely given as God's gift through Jesus Christ, cannot be earned but only received, calling the believer to respond with praise and a life offered back to God.

context: I'm mindful of the truth of Romans 12
point_summary: Receiving the gift of eternal life calls for a response of the whole life as a living sacrifice, as Romans 12:1 frames the believer's gratitude not merely in praise but in consecrated living.

context: Romans 12 verses 1 and 2
point_summary: In response to receiving the unearned gift of eternal life, the believer's whole life—not merely praise—becomes the fitting offering back to God, as Paul's "therefore" in Romans 12:1 grounds consecration in the grace established throughout Romans.

context: Romans 12 verses 1 and 2
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: all have sinned and come short of the glory of God
point_summary: Romans 3:23 frames humanity's universal sinfulness and hopelessness as the necessary backdrop against which the gift of eternal life through Christ becomes intelligible and precious.

context: There's none righteous, no not one
point_summary: Romans 3:10's declaration that none is righteous serves here as part of a survey of Romans establishing humanity's hopeless condition before the gift of salvation through Christ.

context: sin entered into this world by one man, by Adam, and sin has passed upon all men
point_summary: Sin's universal entry through Adam (Romans 5:12) is set alongside Christ's gift as the contrasting remedy, establishing mankind's hopeless condition before the grace offered freely through the cross.

context: by the gift of one, speaking of the price that was paid on the cross of Calvary by the Lord Jesus Christ
point_summary: Against the backdrop of universal sin through Adam, the free gift of Christ's sacrifice on the cross opens salvation to all men without exception.

context: Romans 6, we looked at verse 23 earlier
point_summary: Eternal life through Jesus Christ is God's gift freely available to all, set against the backdrop of sin's universal condemnation and the wages it earns.

context: wages of sin is death, the gift of God is eternal life.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Romans 10, for whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: for all have sinned and come short of the glory of God.
point_summary: Universal human sinfulness and the utter absence of righteousness, summarized in "all have sinned and come short of the glory of God," establishes the hopeless condition from which the gift of eternal life rescues mankind.

context: There's none righteous, no not one,
point_summary: Universal human sinfulness — "none righteous, no not one" — establishes the hopeless condition from which the gift of eternal life through Christ rescues every person.

context: Romans 12 and Paul says, I beseech you therefore, brethren, by the mercies of God, that you present your bodies a living sacrifice, wholly acceptable unto God, which is your reasonable service.
point_summary: Romans 12:1 is presented as the logical response to the preceding chapters' gospel arc—because sin has been addressed and the gift received, presenting one's body as a living sacrifice is the reasonable next step.

context: Be not conformed to this world, but be transformed by the renewing of your mind, that you may prove what is that good and acceptable and perfect will of God.
point_summary: Romans 12:2 is presented as the pivot point where Paul moves believers from understanding their salvation to the call of consecrated living—presenting themselves as living sacrifices in response to God's mercies.

context: Paul says, this is your reasonable service, that we present our bodies a living sacrifice.
point_summary: Presenting one's body as a living sacrifice (Rom. 12:1) is the result of understanding salvation's gift, not a means of obtaining or retaining it.

context: this gift of God as eternal life, as we found in Romans 6
point_summary: Eternal life as the gift of God frames the woman's misunderstanding: she grasped only physical thirst, not the spiritual salvation Romans 6 identifies as God's gift.

context: if you've not received the gift of God, which is eternal life
point_summary: Eternal life is identified as the gift of God—freely available, already paid for, and offered to all who will receive it.

context: The Bible says that we're to be not conformed to this world, but to be transformed by the renewing of our mind, that we may prove what is that good and acceptable and perfect will of God in Romans chapter number 12.
point_summary: Romans 12's command not to be conformed to the world but transformed by the renewing of the mind is cited to establish that Egypt and Pharaoh typologically represent the world and its system.

context: In Romans 10 is a familiar passage of scripture. We think about missions.
point_summary: Romans 10 is introduced as the scriptural foundation for missions, with the urgency grounded in the certainty of eternity and humanity's destiny in either heaven or the lake of fire.

context: The Bible says you're there in Romans 10.
point_summary: Giving to missions—whether financially or through ministry like assembling Romans booklets—accrues as "fruit to your account" before God, making every individual contributor a participant in souls saved and churches planted.

context: We come to Romans 10. We'll look at just a few verses of scripture this evening. It's a passage of scripture that is quite familiar to many of us. In Romans chapter number 10, beginning in verse number 13,
point_summary: The universal promise of Romans 10:13—that whosoever calls on the Lord shall be saved—anchors the sermon's opening case for why missions exists and matters.

context: for whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved.
point_summary: Verse 13 is presented as a precious, universal promise—"whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved"—anchoring the entire passage's argument for why missions is necessary.

context: how then shall they call on him in whom they have not believed? And how shall they believe in him of whom they have not heard? And how shall they hear without a preacher?
point_summary: Salvation's promise in verse 13 creates an urgent logical chain in verse 14: calling requires belief, belief requires hearing, and hearing requires a preacher—establishing the indispensable necessity of missions.

context: how beautiful are the feet of them that preach the gospel of peace and bring glad tidings of good things.
point_summary: Sending preachers is the necessary precondition for beautiful gospel proclamation, as the rhetorical chain of Romans 10:13–17 makes plain in this passage.

context: So then faith cometh by hearing, and hearing by the word of God.
point_summary: Faith's arrival depends entirely on hearing, and hearing depends on the Word of God — establishing the indispensable link between the preached Word and saving faith that grounds the entire missions enterprise.

context: for whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved
point_summary: Romans 10:13 is presented as a precious, universal promise—"whosoever" means anyone without exception—and its certainty rests on God's inability to lie, guaranteeing salvation to all who call.

context: God said, for whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved.
point_summary: The universal promise of Romans 10:13 — that *whosoever* calls upon the Lord *shall* be saved — is an unconditional, certain guarantee rooted in the character of a God who cannot lie.

context: name of the Lord shall be saved
point_summary: Eternal security grounded in Romans 10:13 — that God always keeps His word to those who call on Him — is presented as the confident foundation from which believers can witness to others.

context: How shall they preach except they be sent?
point_summary: Sending missionaries requires financial backing, and without churches providing that support, the sent ones cannot reach the field to preach.

context: how shall they hear without a preacher?
point_summary: Sending missionaries requires financial support from churches, because without being sent they cannot preach, and without a preacher the lost cannot hear the gospel.

context: leading back to the promise, that precious promise of verse 13
point_summary: The universal promise of salvation in Romans 10:13 serves as the anchor to which the missionary chain of Romans 10:14–15 leads back, grounding the urgency of sending and preaching.

context: how shall they hear without a preacher?
point_summary: Sending preachers is the indispensable prerequisite to hearing the gospel, because places void of any gospel presence remain in darkness until a preacher arrives.

context: how shall they preach except they be sent?
point_summary: Sending missionaries is the indispensable step that makes gospel hearing possible, since without that act of sending, people in places void of any church or gospel witness have no means of hearing.

context: for whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved
point_summary: The universal promise of salvation for "whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord" creates the urgent problem driving the four questions: unreached villages cannot call on One they have never heard.

context: how then shall they call on Him in whom they have not believed?
point_summary: The four rhetorical questions of verses 14–15, illustrated by a Mexican village with Coca-Cola but no gospel witness, expose the unbroken chain of necessity linking salvation to sending, preaching, hearing, and belief.

context: How shall they believe in Him of whom they have not heard? And how shall they hear without a preacher?
point_summary: Salvation requires prior belief, and belief requires prior hearing, making the chain of Romans 10:14's four rhetorical questions the biblical case for why missions is necessary.

context: How shall they preach except they be sent?
point_summary: Sending is the indispensable link in the chain: without it, there is no preacher, no hearing, no belief, and no calling on the Lord for salvation.

context: so then faith cometh by hearing and hearing by the word of God
point_summary: Faith's arrival depends entirely on hearing, and hearing depends on the Word of God being proclaimed — making the preached gospel the indispensable link between lostness and salvation.

context: They have not all obeyed the gospel
point_summary: Verse 16's sobering acknowledgment that not all will obey the gospel is cited as a realistic counterweight to missions zeal: rejection is expected, but the mandate to keep proclaiming remains.

context: How shall they call on Him in whom they've not believed?
point_summary: Saving faith requires a chain of human transmission—calling, believing, hearing, preaching, sending—and every believer exists because someone faithfully carried the gospel to their family line.

context: How shall they believe in Him of whom they've not heard? How shall they hear without a preacher?
point_summary: Missions giving is framed not as a budget line item but as an eternal investment, grounded in the chain of Romans 10:14—no calling without belief, no belief without hearing, no hearing without a sent preacher.

context: How shall they preach except they be sent?
point_summary: Sending is the indispensable link in the chain of salvation, making financial investment in missions an act of eternal consequence rather than a budget line item.

context: they quoted Romans 1 in verse 16, for I'm not ashamed of the gospel of Christ
point_summary: Romans 1:16's declaration "I am not ashamed of the gospel" is situated within a context of readiness and indebtedness (vv. 14–15) that frames gospel proclamation as purposeful obligation, not passive privilege.

context: That's why Paul says in Romans chapter number one, I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ. Why? For it is the power of God unto salvation to everyone that believeth, to the Jew first and also to the Greek.
point_summary: The gospel's power to save—not human strategy or passive expectation—is what compels the church's active obedience to the Great Commission's first mandate of preaching.

context: raised to walk in newness of life
point_summary: Believer's baptism is framed as the first act of public obedience and identification with Christ, with Romans 6:4's "raised to walk in newness of life" cited as its scriptural picture.

context: all things work together for good, to them who love God, to them who are the called according to His purpose
point_summary: Even when circumstances appear merely unfortunate—slavery, false accusation, imprisonment—God is actively working all things together for good according to His purpose, not that all things are good, but that He works them together for good.

context: the Bible tells us in Romans chapter number five as it references that account of Adam and Eve in the garden in Genesis chapter number three
point_summary: Romans 5 is cited to ground the necessity of Christ's substitutionary death in the universal transmission of sin and its penalty—death—from Adam to all mankind.

context: to all men for that all have sinned
point_summary: Sin's entry through Adam and its universal consequence of death, as drawn from Genesis 3 and Romans 5:12, is presented as the reason Christ's substitutionary death was necessary.

context: for the wages of sin is death
point_summary: Death's wages required a perfect substitute, and Christ's willing self-offering on the cross satisfied sin's penalty so that sinners could be reconciled to God.

context: the wage of sin is death, but I understood that Jesus Christ, God in flesh, He loved me, and He died for me, and He offered me a gift, and that gift is eternal life through Jesus Christ, our Lord.
point_summary: Death as the wage of sin is cited from Romans 6:23 to establish the penalty that Christ's propitiatory death satisfied on behalf of sinners.

context: whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved.
point_summary: The universal scope of Christ's propitiatory work grounds the invitation that "whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved," extending salvation's offer to any unsaved hearer present.

context: it's the gift of God which is eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord
point_summary: Eternal life through Jesus Christ is presented as the gift of God—the offer of Romans 6:23—extended as a final appeal to any unsaved hearer before the closing prayer.

reference_text: for the wages of sin is death
context: for the wages of sin is death and I knew I was guilty, but I had not yet trusted Christ as my savior.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: He has determined in Romans 8, 29 that we are to be conformed to the image of his dear son.
point_summary: Romans 8:29 is cited as the scriptural basis for progressive sanctification—God's determined purpose that believers be conformed to the image of His Son as they live daily by His commands.

context: the just shall live by faith
point_summary: God's definition of true success—rooted in Joshua 1:8's call to meditate on and obey Scripture—connects to the New Testament principle that "the just shall live by faith," meaning genuine prosperity flows from taking God at His word.

context: They did not want to acknowledge God, Romans chapter number one, and God gave them over to a reprobate mind.
point_summary: Romans 1's account of God giving humanity over to a reprobate mind is cited as the explanation for how society arrived at gender confusion — the consequence of refusing to acknowledge God.

context: he's speaking to them about baptism, and he says this in verses three and four of Romans six. Know ye not that so many of us, as were baptized into Jesus Christ, were baptized into his death?
point_summary: Baptism by immersion pictures the death, burial, and resurrection of Christ, making the mode itself inseparable from the ordinance's meaning as a local church symbol.

context: Notice verse number four.
point_summary: Baptism by immersion pictures the death, burial, and resurrection of Christ, making the mode essential to the ordinance's meaning as a visible symbol.

context: we, as many of us as were baptized into Jesus Christ, were baptized into his death
point_summary: Baptism, as the second ordinance of the local church, pictures the death, burial, and resurrection of Jesus Christ, with the believer's immersion enacting union with Christ in his death and rising to newness of life.

context: Therefore, we are buried with him by baptism into death. That like as Christ was raised up from the dead by the glory of the Father
point_summary: Baptism pictures the death, burial, and resurrection of Christ, as the immersion and rising of the believer visually enacts union with him in death and newness of life.

context: Romans chapter number 1 says, professing themselves to be wise, they became fools.
point_summary: Those who claim worldly wisdom while rejecting God's truth are exposed as fools, undercutting any intimidation believers might feel before credentialed false teachers.

context: God's turned them over to a reprobate mind, according to Romans 1.
point_summary: Romans 1's "reprobate mind" is invoked as the divine explanation for why false teachers boldly suppress and distort basic revealed truth about God and creation.

context: In fact, we are more than conquerors. We are on the winning side.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: as Romans 6 and verse 14 tells us, that we're no longer under the law, but we're under grace.
point_summary: Romans 6:14 is cited to establish the sermon's controlling contrast: believers today live under grace, not law, marking the transition from the Mosaic system that anchors the 2 Corinthians 3 passage.

context: What shall we say then? Is the law sin?
point_summary: The law's purpose is not condemnation to be discarded, but revelation — showing sinners their sin, as Paul testifies he would not have known lust apart from the commandment.

context: I had not known sin, but by the law. For I had not known lust, except the law had said, thou shalt not covet.
point_summary: The law's purpose is not condemnation for its own sake but revelation — it exposes sin and shows man he is a sinner, which is why it cannot simply be discarded.

context: But sin, taking occasion by the commandment, wrought in me all manner of concupiscence, for without the law, sin was dead.
point_summary: The law's purpose is not condemnation for its own sake but revelation — exposing sin's true nature and making it "exceeding sinful" in the sinner's awareness.

context: For I was alive without the law once, but when the commandment came, sin revived and I died.
point_summary: The law's purpose is to reveal sin and expose the sinner's true condition, as Paul's testimony that "sin revived and I died" when the commandment came demonstrates.

context: The commandment, which was ordained to life, I found to be unto death.
point_summary: The law's purpose is not condemnation for its own sake but revelation — exposing sin's true nature and showing man his need, so that sin becomes "exceeding sinful" by the commandment.

context: For sin, taking occasion by the commandment, deceived me and by it slew me.
point_summary: The law's purpose is not condemnation as an end in itself, but revelation — exposing sin's true, exceeding sinfulness through the commandment, proving mankind's need for something greater.

context: Wherefore, the law is holy and the commandment holy and just and good.
point_summary: The law's holiness and goodness are affirmed precisely because its purpose is to expose and magnify the exceeding sinfulness of sin, not to be discarded simply because it condemns.

context: Was then that which is good made death unto me? God forbid, but sin, that it might appear sin, working death in me by that which is good, that sin by the commandment might become, notice, exceeding sinful.
point_summary: The law's purpose is not condemnation for its own sake, but to expose sin as "exceeding sinful," making transgression fully visible where without the law it would remain unseen.

context: Jesus said, as it's recorded for us, by Paul writing in Romans chapter 10, for whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved.

context: We mentioned Romans chapter number 7, and we looked at that passage dealing with the law condemning

point_summary: Sanctification—being conformed to the image of Christ (Romans 8:29)—is identified as the ongoing transformation described in 2 Corinthians 3:18, as believers are changed from glory to glory through time in God's presence.

context: Paul says in Romans 7, I had not known sin except for the law said, thou shalt not covet.

context: whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved

context: In Romans chapter number five, if you want to take the time to turn there, the Bible reveals to us in that portion of Scripture this truth that we're dealing with this morning, the reason for Christ's suffering. Some are saved by the grace of God and some are still lost. We're all sinners. Someone asked the question, what makes you a sinner? Is it because you sin? No. Though that could be true that you could say that, but the real truth, the root of the matter is that we sin because we're sinners. And in Romans five and verse number 12, the Bible says it this way, wherefore as by one man, sin entered into the world.
point_summary: Through Adam's one act of disobedience, sin and death passed universally upon all humanity, making sinfulness not merely a behavior but the root condition of every person—the very problem Christ came to remedy.

context: the wages of sin is death, but what a great conjunction, the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ, our Lord

context: for the gospel is the power of God unto salvation to everyone that believeth, to the Jew first and also to the Greek

context: Now I beseech you, brethren, mark them which cause divisions and offenses contrary to what? To the doctrine which ye have learned, and avoid them.

context: for they that are such serve not our Lord Jesus Christ, but

context: The Bible says in Romans chapter 10 and verse number 13, for whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved.

context: He did not say that the gift of God is probationary life. He did not say that the gift of God is temporary life. He said that the gift of God is eternal life.

context: the truth of Romans 8 28 we say hey listen brother we know that God works all things for good right but when we're going through the situation man we just somehow can't remember Romans 8 28
point_summary: Even truths we readily offer others—like God working all things for good—can be hardest to apply when we ourselves are the ones suffering the hurt.

context: Notice in Romans chapter number 12 in verses 1 and 2, the apostle Paul is writing to these believers. He says, I beseech you, therefore, brethren.
point_summary: Paul's appeal in Romans 12:1 is addressed specifically to believers already saved, not to the lost, establishing that the call to surrender is a post-conversion obligation for those who belong to Christ.

reference_text: I beseech you, therefore, brethren, by the mercies of God that you present your bodies a living sacrifice, holy, acceptable unto God, which is your reasonable service
context: Notice it says, I beseech you, therefore, brethren, by the mercies of God that you present your bodies a living sacrifice, holy, acceptable unto God, which is your reasonable service
point_summary: Romans 12:1 is addressed specifically to believers—identified by Paul as "brethren"—calling them, not the unsaved, to present their bodies as a living sacrifice in absolute surrender to God.

reference_text: And be not conformed to this world, but be transformed by the renewing of your mind that you may prove what is that good and acceptable and perfect will of God
context: And be not conformed to this world, but be transformed by the renewing of your mind that you may prove what is that good and acceptable and perfect will of God
point_summary: Romans 12:2 is addressed specifically to believers—not the lost—calling them to a surrendered life of non-conformity and mental transformation as their ongoing response to salvation.

context: I beseech you therefore brethren by the mercies of God
point_summary: Paul's plea in Romans 12:1 — "I beseech you therefore" — is grounded in all that has been previously revealed in Romans, making total surrender to God nothing more than a "reasonable service" in light of those mercies.

context: for all have sinned and come short of the glory of God
point_summary: Universal human guilt before God—established in Romans 3 as the theological foundation for Paul's plea in Romans 12—means every person falls short of Christ's perfection, not merely another person's standard.

context: As it is written, there is none righteous, no, not one
point_summary: Universal human guilt before God—established in Romans 3 as the theological foundation for why surrendered living in Romans 12 is merely reasonable, not exceptional.

context: For the wages of sin is death.
point_summary: Drawn from Romans 6:23's declaration that "the wages of sin is death," the sermon presses that this penalty extends beyond physical death to eternal judgment—the second death in the lake of fire.

context: Paul, he says to these people in Rome that we're all guilty sinners in chapter number three.
point_summary: Universal human guilt established in Romans 3 grounds the entire gospel arc: without the diagnosis that all are sinners deserving death, the gift of eternal life in chapters 5–6 would carry no weight as good news.

context: In chapters five and six, he says that the penalty, the just penalty for sin is death.
point_summary: Romans 5–6 frames the gospel's two-part structure: humanity's just penalty is death, but God's contrasting gift—unearned, unlike a wage—is eternal life through Christ's death, burial, and resurrection.

context: In chapters five and six, he says that the penalty, the just penalty for sin is death.
point_summary: Romans 6 is presented as the hinge between humanity's just condemnation—the wage of sin being death—and the contrasting gift of eternal life through Christ's death, burial, and resurrection.

reference_text: the gift of God is eternal life
context: he says, what a wonderful truth, the gift of God is eternal life.

context: The wage of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ, our Lord.
point_summary: The wage-versus-gift contrast in Romans 6:23 is used to show that death is what sin earns, while eternal life—made possible through Christ's death, burial, and resurrection—is an unmerited divine gift.

reference_text: the wage of sin is death
context: We understand that we're guilty sinners and the payment is death, the wage of sin is death, but we understand that Jesus Christ died for us.
point_summary: Eternal life is a gift received, not a wage earned — God paid the full cost through Christ, and the sinner's only part is to receive it.

context: for whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved
point_summary: Salvation is a free gift requiring only reception — "whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved" — yet many tragically refuse what cost Jesus everything.

context: for whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved
point_summary: Salvation through Christ is a free gift that cannot be earned, offered universally to "whosoever" calls on the Lord's name, making surrender to him the only reasonable response.

context: the wage, the payment for that sin is death, but Jesus Christ died for you and he offers you this free gift

context: I beseech you, therefore, brethren, by the mercies of God that you present your bodies a living sacrifice, holy, acceptable unto God, which is your reasonable service

context: for in that he died, he died unto sin once, but in that he liveth, he liveth unto God.

context: Likewise, reckon ye also yourselves to be dead indeed unto sin, but alive unto God through Jesus Christ our Lord.

context: Let not sin therefore reign in your mortal body that you should obey it in the lust thereof.

context: Neither yield ye your members as instruments of unrighteousness unto sin, but yield yourselves unto God.

context: Know ye not that to whom ye yield yourselves, servants to obey, his servants ye are to whom you obey, whether of sin unto death or of obedience unto righteousness.
point_summary: Yielding oneself to God rather than to sin determines whose servant one becomes, making surrender to God the path to obedience and righteousness.

context: What shall we say then that Abraham our father, as pertaining to the flesh, hath found?

context: For if Abraham were justified by works, he hath whereof to glory, but not before God.
point_summary: Justification by works would give Abraham grounds for boasting before men, but Paul's point—reinforced by Ephesians 2:8–9—is that salvation by grace through faith eliminates all human boasting before God.

context: And so in verse number 2 of Romans 4, here's what Paul is saying to these believers. He says, if Abraham were justified by works, he hath whereof to glory, but not before God.
point_summary: Justification by works, if it were possible, would give Abraham grounds for boasting before men, but Paul's point is that no such boasting stands before God, since salvation is by grace through faith alone.

context: now to him that worketh is the reward not reckoned of grace, but of debt

context: but to him that worketh not, but believeth on him that justifieth the ungodly, his faith is counted for righteousness

context: now the God of hope fill you with all joy and peace in believing that you may abound in hope through the power of the Holy Ghost.

context: God said that all have sinned and come short of the glory of God.

point_summary: Romans 8:39 is read as the climactic conclusion of one of only three biblical occurrences of the phrase "love of Christ," establishing the inseparability of believers from that love as the sermon's foundational text.

context: There is none righteous, no, not one
point_summary: Romans 3:10's declaration that "there is none righteous, no, not one" marks the first crescendo of the book's descent into universal human sinfulness, setting the doctrinal foundation from which Romans builds toward salvation.

context: for all have sinned and come short of the glory of God
point_summary: Romans 3:23 is cited as the first doctrinal crescendo in Romans, marking the conclusion of Paul's indictment of all humanity—heathen, hypocrite, and Hebrew—as universally sinful and without excuse.

context: I beseech you, brethren, by the mercies of God, et cetera, present your bodies a living sacrifice
point_summary: Romans 12:1's call to present one's body a living sacrifice is the practical capstone built upon the doctrinal foundation laid across Romans 1–11, moving from humanity's utter sinfulness to consecrated response.

context: it speaks in verse 18 of all ungodliness and the unrighteousness of men
point_summary: Romans 1:18 marks the starting point of Paul's descent into humanity's "dark depth," introducing the universal ungodliness and unrighteousness that the subsequent verses build upon to expose the utter sinfulness of mankind.

context: It tells us in verse 19, they are without excuse
point_summary: Humanity's inexcusable guilt before God is the opening depth from which Romans traces the full descent into ungodliness, vain imagination, and moral abandonment.

context: It tells us in verse 21, they glorified Him not as God, neither were thankful, but became vain in their imagination and their foolish hearts was darkened
point_summary: Romans 1:21 is cited as one step in a descending chain of human depravity—ungrateful refusal to glorify God darkens the heart and initiates the progressive moral collapse catalogued through the rest of the chapter.

context: also gave them up to uncleanness through the lust of their own hearts. I'm in verse 24, to dishonor their own bodies between themselves.
point_summary: God's abandonment of humanity to the lusts of their own hearts, resulting in self-dishonor, is presented as one cumulative step in Romans 1's escalating exposure of mankind's utter sinfulness before God.

context: In verse 25, it tells them they worshiped and served the creature more than the Creator.
point_summary: Worshiping and serving the creature more than the Creator is cited as one progressive step in Romans 1's cumulative exposure of humanity's utter sinfulness before God.

context: It tells us in verse 26 that they had vile affections.
point_summary: Romans 1:26's description of "vile affections" is cited as one progressive step in a cumulative portrait of humanity's utter sinfulness before God, building toward the depths from which salvation rescues.

context: It says in verse 28 that God gave them over to a reprobate mind.
point_summary: Romans 1:28's declaration that God gave humanity over to a reprobate mind represents one descending step in a cumulative cascade of sin that defines the lost condition every believer once shared.

context: And verses 29 to 32 gives one area of sin, one sin after another after another in one of the deep sin lists.
point_summary: Romans 1:29–32 is presented as one of Scripture's concentrated "sin lists," cataloguing successive transgressions that together expose the utter depth of lostness shared by all humanity before salvation.

context: then go on from chapter one to chapter two and see thou that judgest.
point_summary: Romans 2:1's rebuke of the one who judges warns the self-described moral person that self-righteousness places them under the same guilt catalogued in Romans 1's sin list.

context: Who shall separate me from the love of Christ?
point_summary: No circumstance of suffering or personal failure can sever the believer from Christ's love, making Romans 8:35's rhetorical question a source of confidence that displaces self-reliance entirely.

context: as it is written, for thy sake we are killed all the day long. We are accounted as sheep for the slaughter.
point_summary: Stopping at Romans 8:36's bleak image of sheep for slaughter distorts the passage; the verse must be read in its full context, which moves believers from apparent victimhood to victory in Christ's unbreakable love.

context: Nay, in all these things we are more than conquerors through him that loved us
point_summary: Confidence in the love of Christ—not self-confidence—is what enables believers to declare "more than conquerors" amid tribulation, depression, broken homes, and every circumstance the devil uses to undermine assurance.

context: For I am persuaded, I have been convinced absolutely, and therefore I have absolute confidence that neither death, nor life, nor angels, nor principalities, etc.
point_summary: Confidence in the love of Christ—not in self or circumstances—is the ground of Paul's persuasion that nothing whatsoever can separate believers from that love.

context: nor height, nor depth, etc., shall be able to separate us from the love of God
point_summary: Unshakeable confidence in the love of God—not self-confidence—is the ground on which believers stand against every trial, attack, and circumstance that would threaten to separate them from Christ.

context: Paul said in Romans 1.16, I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ, for it is the power of God unto salvation, to everyone that believe it, to the Jew first, and also to the Greek.
point_summary: The gospel's power to save sinners continues undiminished even amid pandemic restrictions, as evidenced by souls coming to faith during a season when normal church and outreach ministries were halted.

context: Romans 10, in verse number 9, the Bible says that if thou shalt confess with thy mouth the Lord Jesus, and shalt believe in thine heart that God hath raised Him from the dead, thou shalt be saved.
point_summary: Even amid pandemic restrictions on ministry, the gospel retains its saving power, as Romans 10:9 promises that confession and heart-belief in Christ's resurrection unfailingly brings salvation.

context: It says in verse number 13, for whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved.
point_summary: The universal promise of Romans 10:13 — that whosoever calls on the Lord shall be saved — grounds the assurance that even amid pandemic disruptions to gospel ministry, Jesus is still saving sinners.

context: for whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved
point_summary: The universal promise of Romans 10:13 — that whosoever calls on the Lord's name shall be saved — is presented as still actively operative, with an urgent appeal for any unsaved listener to act without delay.

reference_text: whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved

context: Look in Romans chapter number eight, if you will.
point_summary: Salvation quickens the believer's spirit, initiating an ongoing conflict between flesh and spirit that Romans 8 addresses as the foundational principle for how a Christian can practically please God.

context: so then they that are in the flesh, notice what it says, can not please God.
point_summary: Those living according to the flesh cannot please God, making the daily battle against fleshly desires a defining challenge for the believer seeking to walk in the Spirit.

context: the Bible says in Romans 8 and verse number 8, that if we're in the flesh, we can not please God
point_summary: Those who are in the flesh cannot please God, making the avoidance of fleshly living a non-negotiable condition for anyone seeking to please the Lord.

context: for to be carnally minded is death, but to be spiritually minded is life and peace
point_summary: Carnal-mindedness leads to death while spiritual-mindedness yields life and peace — a stark contrast Paul uses to establish that walking in the flesh makes pleasing God impossible.

context: ...We mentioned already in Romans 8 about walking in the flesh...
point_summary: Walking in the flesh is cited as a clear biblical example of what does not please God, contrasting with the Ephesians 5 call to prove what is well-pleasing to the Lord.

context: Well, the Bible says, so then faith cometh by hearing, and hearing by the
point_summary: Faith is produced by hearing God's Word—meaning time in Scripture is the necessary means by which a believer's capacity to take God at His word is built and sustained.

context: the gift of God, which is eternal life through Jesus Christ, our Lord
point_summary: Eternal life is presented as a divine gift — the specific language of Romans 6:23 — offered to the unsaved as the only remedy where the Lord's Supper elements themselves cannot save.

context: Paul said, I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ. Why? For it is the power of God unto salvation to everyone that believe it, to the Jew first and also to the Greek
point_summary: The gospel's power to save every believer—Jew and Greek alike—is cited as the reason Christians cultivate a passion for sharing salvation with the lost.

context: The wage of sin is death.
point_summary: The wage of sin is death — a truth that, when grasped under the Spirit's conviction, drives a lost person to call on the Lord for the gift of salvation.

context: For whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved.
point_summary: Soul-winning is presented as the believer's supreme earthly purpose, with Romans 10:13 grounding the invitation to "call upon the name of the Lord" as the moment of salvation that makes witnessing eternally worthwhile.

context: romans one talks about those that try to deny the existence of god

context: ...for all have sinned and come short of the glory of god...
point_summary: The phrase "all have sinned and come short of the glory of God" is cited to establish that the statement "Christ came to save sinners" reveals the universal lost condition of mankind.

context: for whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved
point_summary: Romans 10:13 is cited as the open invitation grounding the gospel appeal: because Christ came to save sinners, anyone who calls on the Lord's name can have their name written in the book of life today.

context: Romans 3 and verse number 10, as it is written, there's none righteous, no, not one, for all have sinned and come short of the glory of God.
point_summary: Universal human depravity is not a modern development but the constant condition of mankind from the beginning, as Romans 3:10 declares that none are righteous—not one.

context: Wherefore, as by one man sin entered into the world, and death by sin, and so death passed upon all men, for that all have sinned.
point_summary: Romans 5:12 is cited to establish that universal human depravity is not a modern development but the inherited condition of all mankind since sin entered the world through one man.

context: Notice what the Bible says in Romans chapter number 14 with regard to this truth of this last phrase there in verse number 16 with regard to the trap of legalism. And in Romans chapter number 14, in verse number 5, many still turning, give you a moment there, Romans 14 and verse number 5.
point_summary: Observance of special days is a matter of personal conviction, not a binding standard by which believers may judge one another, as Paul's transitional context between Jewish and Gentile practice makes clear.

context: back to Romans 14, verse number 5. Notice what he says, one man esteemeth one day above another, another esteemeth every day alike. Let every man be fully persuaded in his own mind.
point_summary: Observance of special days belongs to a transitional era now fulfilled in Christ, and believers are not to be judged over such matters since each must be fully persuaded in his own mind.

context: Look, if you will, at verse 13. It says, "...let us not therefore judge one another any more, but judge this rather, that no man put a stumbling block for an occasion to fall in his brother's way."
point_summary: Rather than judging fellow believers over disputable matters, the call is to ensure no one becomes a stumbling block that causes a brother to fall — a principle bridging legalism's trap to the Corinthian discussion of conscience.

context: ...the truth shall make you free...
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

reference_text: For all have sinned and come short of the glory of God
context: For all have sinned and come short of the glory of God.
point_summary: Sin's universality — "all have sinned and come short of the glory of God" — is invoked to establish why Christ's death was necessary: every person stands guilty before God.

context: the truth of Romans chapter number five, the Bible speaks about that as sin entered in the world, that death also is the result.
point_summary: Sin's entry into the world through the Fall brought both physical and spiritual death as its consequence, establishing humanity's universal need for a savior whose blood alone satisfies the debt.

context: Paul, a servant of Jesus Christ, called to be an apostle, notice what it says, separated unto the gospel of God
point_summary: Paul's self-identification as one "separated unto the gospel of God" grounds the sermon's claim that the resurrection compelled him to preach—because Christ lives, the gospel mission becomes inescapable obligation.

context: so, as much as in me is, I am ready to preach the gospel to you that are at Rome also
point_summary: Paul's declaration of readiness to preach the gospel in Rome is presented as flowing directly from his conviction that Christ is risen and alive.

context: Paul says, I beseech you therefore, brethren, by the mercies of God that you present your bodies a living sacrifice, holy, acceptable unto God, which is your reasonable service
point_summary: Presenting one's body as a living sacrifice—the New Testament fulfillment of Old Testament altar offerings—is the defining mark of a believer who lives for Christ rather than self.

context: and be not conformed to this world, but be transformed by the renewing of your mind that you may prove what is that good and acceptable and perfect will of God
point_summary: Presenting one's body as a living sacrifice and refusing conformity to the world is the reasonable, expected response of believers to God's mercy, connecting Old Testament altar imagery to New Testament consecration.

context: I beseech you therefore, brethren, by the mercies of God, that you present your bodies a living sacrifice, holy, acceptable unto God, which is your reasonable service
point_summary: Presenting oneself as a living sacrifice to God, as Romans 12:1 calls believers to do, is framed as the foundational act that makes "for me to live is Christ" a livable daily reality.

reference_text: the wage of sin is death
context: Because the Bible says the wage of sin is death.
point_summary: Death entered the world through sin (Gen. 3; Rom. 5), making sin the sting of death, as Romans 6:23's wage-of-sin-is-death principle explains the origin of human mortality.

context: Well, the Bible tells us that that's when death entered into the world. In Romans chapter number five, you can read the portion of Scripture
point_summary: Death entered the world through Adam's sin in Genesis 3, making sin the sting of death—a consequence traceable to one act of disobedience against God's single command.

context: as by one man, that's speaking of Adam, sin entered into the world, and death by sin.
point_summary: Death entered the world through Adam's sin in Genesis 3, and Romans 5:12 is cited to establish that physical and spiritual death are the universal consequence of that one act of disobedience.

context: The wages of sin is death.
point_summary: Death entered the world through Adam's sin, and Romans 6:23's "wages of sin is death" encompasses both physical and spiritual death—the second death awaiting those born only once.

reference_text: the wages of sin is death
context: because the Bible says that the wages of sin is death
point_summary: Death as the wage of sin is both physical and spiritual, but the new birth reverses that sentence so that those born twice die only once, escaping the second death entirely.

context: Remember, Paul says, I had not known sin if it were not for the law, because the law told me thou shalt not covet, and therefore I knew that I was sinning because I was coveting, Romans chapter number seven.
point_summary: Romans 7's testimony that the law reveals sin—"thou shalt not covet"—is cited briefly to explain Paul's phrase "the strength of sin is the law," without further exposition in this segment.

context: as it is written, Romans 3.10, there's none righteous, no, not one.
point_summary: Romans 3:10 serves as the opening verse in a gospel-presentation sequence establishing that universal sinfulness must be acknowledged before a need for a Savior can be recognized.

context: Romans 3.23, for all have sinned and come short of the glory of God.
point_summary: Romans 3:23 is presented as the essential first step in gospel witness—establishing universal sinfulness as the necessary foundation before a Savior can be introduced.

context: Romans 5.12 deals with the same thing that while God demonstrated his love toward us
point_summary: Romans 5:12 is grouped with Romans 3:10 and 3:23 as gospel-entry verses establishing the universal sinfulness that makes a Savior necessary before any further presentation of the gospel can proceed.

context: while we were yet sinners, Christ died for us
point_summary: Romans 5:8 is cited as part of a sequential Romans-road presentation establishing that Christ's atoning death occurred while humanity was still in its sinful condition, making sin's universality the necessary starting point for the gospel.

context: for all have sinned and come short of the glory of God
point_summary: Universal sinfulness—encompassing every person without exception, including the preacher himself—is the necessary starting point for gospel conversation, because without acknowledged sin there is no need for a Savior.

context: Romans 6.23, for the wages of sin is death. The wages of sin is death. The rest of the verse, but the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord.
point_summary: In evangelism, Romans 6:23a ("the wages of sin is death") is deliberately isolated from the rest of the verse to press the hearer first to accept the reality of sin's penalty before the gospel offer is introduced.

context: ...for the wage of sin is death...
point_summary: Deliberately pausing on "the wages of sin is death" before completing the verse, the sermon treats this phrase as establishing the sinner's guilt and the penalty—physical and eternal spiritual separation from God.

context: if you just start in Romans 10, 13,
point_summary: Presenting Romans 10:13's promise of salvation before establishing sin and its penalty renders the gospel's good news meaningless, since a person must first understand why they need a Savior before they can grasp why they should call on one.

context: but God commendeth his love toward us and that while we're yet sinners, Christ died for us.
point_summary: Christ's death for sinners while they were yet in their sin is presented as the pivotal "good news" that only makes sense after a person has grasped both their guilt and sin's penalty of eternal separation from God.

context: The Bible says in Romans 6, 23, we read the first part in the second acknowledgement
point_summary: Romans 6:23 is presented in two parts—the "wages of sin is death" establishing penalty, and "the gift of God is eternal life" inviting a contrast between what is earned and what is freely given.

context: for the wage of sin is death, but the Bible continues that the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord.
point_summary: Eternal life is God's gift, not a wage earned by human effort—Christ paid for it on the cross, making salvation freely receivable rather than merited.

context: the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord
point_summary: The contrast between "wage" and "gift" in Romans 6:23 is pressed to show that eternal life cannot be earned but must be received, since someone—Christ—already paid for it.

context: for whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved
point_summary: Calling upon the name of the Lord in prayer—acknowledging one's sin and trusting Christ—is presented as the individual's act of receiving the free gift of eternal life.

context: the Bible says that if thou shalt confess with thy mouth the Lord Jesus, and shalt believe in thine heart that God hath raised Him from the dead, thou shalt be saved.
point_summary: Verbal confession of Christ as Lord, paired with heart-belief in His resurrection, constitutes the biblical pattern for receiving salvation—distinct from baptism, church attendance, or any other work.

context: For with the heart man believeth unto righteousness, and with the mouth confession is made unto salvation.
point_summary: Vocal confession of faith in Christ is presented as the outward expression of inward belief, distinguishing salvation by faith alone from works such as baptism, church attendance, or giving.

context: Paul writes, and he says this in Romans 8 and verse 28, and I like the first words, and we know. I'm not sure how often I've heard Romans 8.28 quoted without quoting it completely. So often I've heard Romans 8.28, all things work together for good. But the Bible says, and we know.
point_summary: The often-truncated opening words "and we know" carry the sermon's weight: certainty about God's sovereign plan rests not on circumstance but on revealed knowledge of His character and nature.

context: the Bible says, and we know that all things work together for good to them that love God, to them who are the called according to His purpose.
point_summary: The sermon draws out the often-omitted opening words "and we know" as the theological anchor of Romans 8:28, arguing that this certainty rests on God's revealed character and purpose, not on circumstances being favorable.

context: Now, we made note of Romans eight and 28 and we talked about the start and we know.
point_summary: God's absolute sovereignty — affirmed by the "we know" of Romans 8:28 — means his control over life's events is unconditional, not contingent on circumstances going the way we desire.

context: The Bible tells us in the book of Romans in the fifth chapter that all have sinned.
point_summary: Romans 5's teaching that sin entered through Adam and has passed to all humanity establishes the universal need from which Jesus, whose very name means Savior, came to redeem mankind.

context: for all have sinned and come short of the glory of God
point_summary: Universal sinfulness — "all have sinned and come short of the glory of God" — establishes the sin problem as the greatest and most fundamental need of every person, making salvation through Jesus the only remedy.

context: The Bible says, for whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved.
point_summary: Calling upon the name of the Lord for salvation—cited as the preacher's own testimony of crying out to God in faith 25 years prior—grounds the saving power of Jesus' name in personal experience.

context: every knee is going to bow, and every tongue is going to confess
point_summary: Every knee bowing and every tongue confessing Christ as Lord is not a question of if but of when—and the timing, whether in this life by faith or in judgment, determines one's eternal destiny.

context: with my tongue I confessed that Jesus was Lord
point_summary: Confessing Jesus as Lord and bowing the knee is universal and inevitable — the only question is whether it happens in this life, securing eternal salvation, or in judgment after death.

context: He gave me the gift of God, which is eternal life.
point_summary: Eternal life as the gift of God through Jesus Christ is received by personal faith in Christ alone, and that reception—when it happens, on this side of eternity—determines one's standing at the final judgment.

context: For whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved.
point_summary: The universal scope of salvation — "whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved" — is applied as an open invitation, connecting the name Jesus directly to his saving work from sin.

context: Don't be afraid of Romans 9 and 10.
point_summary: Romans 9, read alongside Ephesians 1, need not be feared as a proof-text for individual election, but understood as affirming that salvation comes only through Jesus Christ.

context: Don't be afraid of Romans 9 and 10.
point_summary: Romans 9–10, like Ephesians 1, need not be feared but properly understood as affirming that God's singular, predetermined way of salvation is through Jesus Christ alone.

context: Paul says this in Romans chapter 10. I think it's a great example for us to know as we think about how this parenthetical statement of giving to us God's will for humanity in the midst of an exhortation on prayer reveals how knowing God's will influences my prayer life.
point_summary: Knowing that God wills all people to be saved directly shaped Paul's intercessory prayer for Israel, modeling how God's revealed will fuels specific, heartfelt petition.

context: Brethren, my heart's desire and prayer to God for Israel is that they might be saved.
point_summary: Paul's heartfelt prayer for Israel's salvation in Romans 10:1 is cited as a biblical model showing how knowing God's will—that lost people be saved—should actively shape a believer's prayer life.

context: And we know that Paul said, I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ. Why? For it, the gospel of Christ, is the power of God unto salvation.
point_summary: Paul's declaration of being unashamed of the gospel grounds the universal scope of salvation — the gospel is God's power unto salvation for everyone who believes, because God wills all men to be saved.

context: for I'm not ashamed of the gospel of Christ
point_summary: Preaching the gospel, as Paul modeled, is itself an act of faith—trusting that the message carries God's power to save, consistent with living by faith in Romans 1:17.

context: for it is the power of God unto salvation to everyone that believe it, to the Jew first and also to the Greek.
point_summary: Paul's faith was demonstrated in action each time he preached the gospel, trusting that it truly is the power of God unto salvation exactly as God declared.

context: for the just shall live by faith.
point_summary: Preaching the gospel was itself an act of faith for Paul, who lived out Romans 1:17's principle that the just shall live by faith by trusting God's word that the gospel is the power of God unto salvation.

reference_text: Romans chapter number 8, in verse number 29
context: And I'm remembering in my personal devotions from Romans chapter number 8, I was reading several chapters there, but in chapter number 8, in verse number 29 that God, he predestinated us to be conformed to the image of his dear son.
point_summary: God's predestinating purpose—conforming believers to the image of Christ—is offered as the divine rationale behind suffering, since Christ himself suffered.

context: I beseech you therefore, brethren, by the mercies of God, that you present your bodies a living sacrifice, wholly acceptable unto God, which is your reasonable service
point_summary: Presenting one's body as a living sacrifice is not merely doctrinal instruction Paul gave others, but a commitment he himself had already embodied, as evidenced by his own endurance through persecution.

context: and be not conformed to this world, but be ye transformed by the renewing of your mind that ye might prove what is that good and perfect and acceptable will of God
point_summary: Enduring persecution requires a transformed mind and a surrendered life—presenting oneself as a living sacrifice—which Paul modeled personally before ever calling others to it.

reference_text: Romans talks about being dead to the flesh, but alive unto God.
context: Romans talks about being dead to the flesh, but alive unto God.
point_summary: Romans 12:1–2 is cited to show that Paul's call to present one's body as a living sacrifice was no abstract command but a standard Paul himself had already met, grounding his credibility to urge Timothy through persecution.

context: Romans eight, we're more than conquerors.
point_summary: Believers possess divine promises of overcoming power—being "more than conquerors"—yet those promises coexist with the biblical expectation of real-world persecution as they live for Christ.

context: So then faith cometh by hearing and hearing by the word of God.
point_summary: Faith grows through engagement with God's Word, as Romans 10:17 is cited to explain how individual believers increase in faith within the local church.

context: I beseech thee, therefore, by the mercies of God, to present your bodies a living sacrifice, holy, acceptable unto God, which is your reasonable service, and be not conformed to this world, but be transformed.
point_summary: The phrase "I beseech thee" in Moses' intercession is linked to Paul's identical appeal in Romans 12:1, highlighting that earnest petition grounded in divine mercy runs through both texts.

context: For all have sinned, come short of the glory of God
point_summary: Romans 3:23 is cited to establish universal human sinfulness — "we were all in the same position" — as the backdrop for Ephesians 2's declaration that God, rich in mercy, pardoned what all equally deserved.

context: See, God tells us that faith cometh by hearing, and hearing by the word of God.
point_summary: Faith comes by hearing God's word, and just as God graciously gave Gideon repeated faith builders, Scripture itself is the means by which God increases the faith of his people today.

reference_text: the gospel is the power of God unto salvation to everyone that believeth
context: You've promised that your word is powerful, and the gospel is the power of God unto salvation to everyone that believeth.
point_summary: God's promise that the gospel is the power of God unto salvation grounds the call for every believer to fulfill their personal responsibility in sharing it with those only they may reach.

context: Notice Romans 5 in verse number 21. Notice what it said, that as sin hath reigned unto death, even so might grace reign through righteousness unto eternal life by Jesus Christ our Lord.
point_summary: Grace reigning over sin through righteousness unto eternal life (Rom. 5:21) establishes the contrast that false teachers in Jude 4 pervert by twisting that same grace into license for unbridled lust.

context: What shall we say then? When we consider this sin versus the grace of God, what shall we say then? Notice what it says, shall we continue in sin that grace may abound?
point_summary: Romans 6:1's rhetorical question — shall we sin that grace may abound? — is used to warn that twisting God's grace into license for sin renders believers ineffective, because God does not use dirty vessels.

context: What's the answer? God forbid. God forbid. How shall we that are dead to sin live any longer therein?
point_summary: Grace is not a license to continue in sin; the rhetorical question "How shall we that are dead to sin live any longer therein?" forecloses any appeal to God's grace as justification for ongoing sinful living.

context: shall we continue in sin that grace may abound? God forbid.
point_summary: Grace is not a license to sin freely, as the sermon invokes Paul's rhetorical question in Romans 6:1 to warn against the false teaching that God's forgiveness permits ongoing, willful sinning.

context: who is it that condemneth? It is Christ that died, yea, rather, that is risen again, who is even at the right hand of God, who also maketh intercession for us.
point_summary: Christ's present ministry at the right hand of God as intercessor—"who also maketh intercession for us"—is cited as the enduring, unchanging blessing that causes the believer's cup to run over.

context: where sin abounds, grace is more
point_summary: Grace does not license sin — the sermon contrasts the misuse of "where sin abounds, grace abounds more" with Titus 2's teaching that grace instructs believers toward godly living and good works.

context: let love be without dissimulation
point_summary: Genuine love, as Romans 12:9 demands, must be sincere and without hypocrisy, and cannot be separated from abhorring evil — meaning true love requires speaking truth rather than passive acceptance.

context: let love be without dissimulation, abhor that which is evil, cleave to that which is good
point_summary: Genuine love, the sermon draws out, must be without hypocrisy ("dissimulation") and inseparably linked to truth-telling, since claiming to love someone while withholding truth reveals an internal contradiction.

context: Romans chapter number 10, so then faith cometh by hearing and hearing by the Word of God
point_summary: Faith grows specifically through hearing the Word of God (Romans 10), meaning consistent engagement with Scripture is the direct means by which a believer's faith increases.

context: the gift of God is eternal life
point_summary: Eternal life is identified as the gift of God, received through faith in Christ's atoning death, burial, and resurrection—a salvation no sinner could earn for himself.

context: In Romans five and verse number eight, the word of God says, but God commendeth his love toward us. And that while we're yet sinners, Christ died for us.
point_summary: God's initiative in sending Christ to die for sinners while they were still enemies establishes the prior relationship that makes walking in the light possible.

context: For whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved.

context: the wages of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ, our Lord.

context: The penalty of sin is this for the wages of sin is death.

context: Paul says that sin shall no longer have dominion over you.
