context: knowing this, that our old man is crucified with him, that the body of sin might be destroyed, that henceforth we should not serve sin.

context: Likewise, reckon ye also yourselves to be dead indeed unto sin, but alive unto God through Jesus Christ our Lord.

context: Let not sin therefore reign in your mortal body, that you should obey it in the lust thereof.
point_summary: Sin's reign in the believer's body is not inevitable—Romans 6:12 is cited as Paul's direct command not to let sin reign, grounded in the believer's reckoned death to sin in Christ.

context: Neither yield ye your members as instruments of unrighteousness unto sin, but yield yourselves unto God as those that are alive from the dead and your members as instruments of righteousness unto God.
point_summary: Believers are called to actively yield their members as instruments of righteousness rather than unrighteousness, because the old man's crucifixion with Christ means sin no longer has dominion over them.

context: for sin shall not have dominion over you, for you're not under the law, but under grace.
point_summary: Because the believer is no longer under law but under grace, sin's dominion is broken—a reality the sermon ties to the paradox of being dead to sin yet alive to God through Christ.

context: likewise, and reckon ye also yourselves to be dead indeed unto sin, but alive unto God through Jesus Christ our Lord.
point_summary: Believers are called to reckon themselves dead to sin but alive to God — a paradox of the Christian life grounded in the co-crucifixion of the old man with Christ described in verse 6.

context: as he said in verse 6, that my old man is crucified with Him, that the body of sin might be destroyed, that we should not serve sin.
point_summary: The co-crucifixion of the believer's old man with Christ is the doctrinal ground establishing that sin's dominion is broken and the Christian is no longer obligated to serve sin.

context: Verse 17, look at it there. Skip down and look at verse 17. But God be thanked. Notice when he says that ye were the servants of sin.

context: Mortify therefore your members which are upon the earth. Romans 7 verse 15. Notice what Paul says.

context: for that which I do, I allow not. For what I would, that do I not. But what I hate, that do I.

context: If then I do that which I would not, I consent under the law that it is good.

context: Now then it is no more I that do it, but sin that dwelleth in me.
point_summary: Even after salvation, indwelling sin remains a warring force in the believer's members, distinct from the regenerate self, making mortification of the flesh necessary.

context: For I know that in me, that is in my flesh, dwelleth no good thing. For to will is present with me, but how to perform that which is good I find not.
point_summary: The flesh contains no good thing, making mortification necessary for the believer who would serve righteousness rather than remain captive to the sin dwelling within.

context: for the good that I would, I do not. But the evil which I would not, that I do.
point_summary: Even after salvation, the flesh remains a warring force that compels believers toward evil they do not want, making mortification of the flesh a necessary ongoing battle.

context: Now if I do that I would not, it is no more I that do it, but sin that dwelleth in me.

context: I find in a law, that when I would do good, evil is present with me.

context: For I delight in the law of God after the inward man,
point_summary: Regenerate believers genuinely delight in God's law in their inner man, yet still experience the flesh warring against that delight—making mortification of the flesh necessary.

context: but I see another law in my members,
point_summary: Indwelling sin wages war against the believer's renewed mind, bringing the flesh into captivity — a conflict Paul personally experienced and every Christian can recognize in themselves.

context: warring against the law of my mind, and bringing me into captivity to the law of sin, which is in my members.

context: O wretched man that I am! Who shall deliver me from the body of this death?
point_summary: Paul's anguished cry — "O wretched man that I am!" — is presented as the universal Christian experience of the flesh's weakness and its ongoing war against the law of God.

context: I thank God through Jesus Christ our Lord. So then with the mind, I myself serve the law of God, but with the flesh, the law of sin.
point_summary: Even as Paul's cry of deliverance through Christ in Romans 7:25 offers genuine hope, the ongoing war between mind and flesh remains real, making mortification of the flesh a present and urgent necessity.

context: O wretched man that I am!
point_summary: Paul's anguished cry — "O wretched man that I am!" — is presented as the universal Christian experience of the flesh's weakness and its ongoing war against the law of God.

context: For I know that in me that is in my flesh dwelleth no good thing.
point_summary: The flesh contains no good thing, making the believer's ongoing struggle against sin not a failure of willpower but an inevitable conflict requiring mortification rather than self-confident exposure to temptation.

context: I beseech you therefore brethren, by the mercies of God, notice that you present your bodies a living sacrifice, wholly acceptable unto God, which is your reasonable service.
point_summary: Presenting the body as a living sacrifice requires actively guarding against conformity to the world's thinking, especially for those already shaped by it before salvation.

context: And be not conformed to this world, but be transformed by the renewing of your mind that you may prove what is that good and acceptable and perfect will of God.
point_summary: Renewing the mind is harder than reverting to worldly thinking, because those saved out of sin already know the world's philosophy and can easily drift back into it.

reference_text: The just shall live by faith
context: The just shall live by faith. How do we have faith?
point_summary: Faith comes by hearing the Word of God, and believers—unlike the fearful Israelites or Canaanites—are called to live by that faith, growing in courage through what Scripture reveals of God's acts.

reference_text: So then faith cometh by hearing, and hearing by the word of God
context: So then faith cometh by hearing, and hearing by the word of God.
point_summary: Faith comes by hearing the Word of God, and believers—unlike the fearful Israelites or the terrified Canaanites—should respond to what they hear with increased faith and courage to press forward.

context: If God before us, who can be against us?
point_summary: The assurance that God is "before us" means believers are on the winning side and need not live defeated, fearful lives in the face of threatening reports.

context: we are more than conquerors through him that loved us
point_summary: Believers are not to live defeated but as "more than conquerors," assured that God's faithfulness enables victory over circumstances through faith rather than sight.

context: The things that I would, I do not. The things that I would not, that I do.
point_summary: Paul's confession in Romans 7:19 is invoked to illustrate the common gap between knowing God's plan and actually doing it, pressing the question of whether believers are doers of the Word.

context: Remember the truth of Romans 8.28 that we so often seek to share with one another when we're dealing with burdens and trials and tribulations. It begins this way, and we know. We don't think, we don't hope, we know that all things work together for good.
point_summary: God's sovereignty over suffering is grounded in the certainty of Romans 8:28—not hope or assumption, but knowledge that all things work together for good, even imprisonment.

context: In Romans chapter number 4, the Bible tells us about Abraham, it tells us really a lot about Abraham in the entire passage, but in verse number 18 of Romans 4, it says,
point_summary: Abraham's faith is invoked as the interpretive lens for why God addressed him—not Sarah—about her laughter, since Romans 4:18 portrays him as the one who believed against hope regarding the very promise Sarah doubted.

context: Who against hope believed in hope, that he might become the father of many nations, according to that which was spoken, so shall thy seed be.
point_summary: Abraham's unwavering faith described in Romans 4:18—believing against hope, not staggering at God's promise—is placed after Genesis 17, marking a turning point beyond his earlier wavering responses.

context: And being not weak in faith, he considered not his own body now dead, when he was about a hundred years old, neither yet the deadness of Sarah's womb.
point_summary: Abraham's unwavering faith described in Romans 4:19 is understood as the posture he reached after Genesis 17, not a description of his entire life.

context: He staggered not at the promise of God through unbelief, but was strong in faith, giving glory to God.
point_summary: Abraham's unwavering faith described in Romans 4:20 is understood here as characterizing him specifically after Genesis 17, not his entire life, following God's decisive reconfirmation of the promise through Sarah.

context: And being fully persuaded that which he had promised, he was able also to perform.
point_summary: Abraham's full persuasion that God could perform what He promised (Rom. 4:21) is placed after Genesis 17:18, marking the point where Abraham's faith finally overcame his pattern of offering alternatives.

context: The Bible says that the wage of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ, our Lord.
point_summary: Death is sin's wage, but the gift of eternal life is free to the recipient precisely because it was purchased by the blood of Jesus Christ.

context: But God commendeth His love toward us, and that while we're yet sinners, Christ died for us
point_summary: God's love for sinners is demonstrated not in spite of Christ's death but precisely through it, grounding the call to share the gospel with those who do not yet know why Jesus died.

context: Romans chapter 1 teaches us what man does to try to define God when they don't want to be accountable to the God who's revealed Himself to us in Scripture.
point_summary: Romans 1 is cited as the biblical witness to humanity's tendency to redefine God on its own terms when unwilling to be accountable to the God who has revealed Himself in Scripture.

context: Romans 1, we're familiar when Paul says, I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ.
point_summary: The declaration "I am not ashamed of the gospel" in Romans 1:16 is set in context of Paul first calling himself a debtor, framing gospel proclamation as a responsibility owed to those who have not yet heard.

context: according to Romans 1, that the people, they want to deny God as being the Creator.
point_summary: Romans 1 is cited as evidence that humanity suppresses the truth of God as Creator, a denial the sermon connects to the broader attack on Genesis 1–11 and the authority of Scripture.

context: ...for rulers are not a terror to good works, but to the evil...
point_summary: Romans 13:3–4 is cited as New Testament confirmation that God-ordained human government bears the sword as His minister to restrain evil and execute wrath, grounding the capital punishment principle established in Genesis 9:6.

context: ...Wilt thou then not be afraid of the power? Do that which is good, and thou shalt have praise of the same.
point_summary: Romans 13:4 is cited as New Testament confirmation that God-ordained government bears the sword as His minister to execute wrath on evildoers, grounding the capital punishment principle established in Genesis 9:6.

context: Romans chapter 1 tells us that they want to deny the existence of the Creator because really they don't want to be accountable to the Creator.
point_summary: Theories that deny God as Creator are motivated, as Romans 1 indicates, by the desire to escape accountability to Him.

context: Professing themselves to be wise. But Romans 1 says they became fools.
point_summary: Those who deny the Creator, though professing wisdom, are exposed by Romans 1 as fools—a charge applied here to those who debate and reject the ordered logic evident in Genesis 1.

context: I say the truth in Christ, I lie not, my conscience also bearing me witness in the Holy Ghost
point_summary: Paul's solemn oath in Romans 9:1 — "I say the truth in Christ, I lie not" — is cited as authenticating the depth of his grief over the lost, connecting his continual sorrow to Elisha's weeping.

context: for I could wish that myself were accursed from Christ for my brethren, my kinsmen according to the flesh.
point_summary: Paul's anguished willingness to be accursed from Christ for his unsaved Jewish kinsmen illustrates that ethnic or religious heritage grants no automatic standing before God.

context: brethren, my heart's desire and prayer to God for Israel is that they might be saved.
point_summary: Paul's anguished, prayerful longing for Israel's salvation illustrates that ethnic or religious heritage grants no automatic standing before God.

context: Paul says, I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ, it is the power of God unto salvation, to the Jew first and also to the Gentile or to the Greek.
point_summary: Paul's declaration of being unashamed of the gospel as God's saving power for Jew and Gentile alike is cited to refute the heresy that Jewish heritage alone secures salvation.

context: He says that I have great heaviness in chapter 9 and verse number 2, continual sorrow in my heart.
point_summary: Paul's declaration of "great heaviness" and "continual sorrow" in Romans 9:2 is held up as the measure of genuine gospel burden—a grief so deep he could wish himself accursed for the sake of lost souls.

context: I could wish that myself were accursed from Christ for my brethren, my kinsmen, according to the flesh.
point_summary: Paul's willingness in Romans 9:3 to be accursed from Christ for his kinsmen's salvation illustrates a depth of burden for the lost that the sermon holds up as a standard for genuine gospel-driven compassion.

context: Let me share a verse with you in Romans chapter number 8.
point_summary: Romans 8 is introduced as a well-known verse more readily applied to counsel others than personally practiced, offered as the scriptural basis for praising God in adversity.

context: In Romans chapter number 8, I want you to consider this great promise in verse 28. I want you to think about these first three words, and we know.
point_summary: The certainty embedded in "and we know" distinguishes confident, character-grounded knowledge of God's sovereignty and plan from mere assumption or opinion, making praise in adversity natural rather than forced.

context: And we find here in Romans 8 and verse 28, and we know, all right? So we made focus on the word, no, but then I wanted you to make focus on the next phrase. And we know that, what's it say? All things, all things.
point_summary: The opening phrase "and we know" in Romans 8:28 is treated as a declaration of certain knowledge—not assumption or opinion—grounded in what believers can confidently affirm about God's character, sovereignty, and plan.

context: we know that all things work together for good. To them, it says this, that love God to them who are the called according to His purpose.
point_summary: God's promise in Romans 8:28 is presented as certain knowledge, not assumption, and its scope—"all things"—applies universally to all who love God and are called according to His purpose, not selectively to some.

context: we understand that man has been created in the image of God that The that not only is God the creator But he is the Redeemer of mankind and just as we'd find in Romans chapter number one We don't time to go there this evening.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: for there's none righteous, no, not one
point_summary: Cited alongside Romans 6:23 to underscore that no believer deserved eternal life, since Scripture declares none righteous and all fall short—mirroring the leprous men who received undeserved provision.

context: For all have sinned and come short of the glory of God
point_summary: All humanity's universal sinfulness and unworthiness—"all have sinned and come short of the glory of God"—establishes that believers, like the four lepers, received eternal life entirely undeserved.

context: for the wages of sin is death. That's what we deserve. But the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ, our Lord.
point_summary: Eternal life is an undeserved gift — what sinners actually deserve, according to Romans 6:23, is death — received by grace just as the lepers received unearned sustenance.

context: we've received the gift of God, which is eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: You read Romans chapter number five, and you find here that the superiority of this new covenant that is mentioned in our passage of Scripture, that it's because Jesus is not only the surety, but he's also the mediator.
point_summary: Romans 5 is invoked as scriptural witness to humanity's enmity with God, which Christ's substitutionary death, burial, and resurrection overcame through his dual role as surety and mediator of the new covenant.

context: Go over to Romans chapter 11 Because really when we get to see all that's happening here.
point_summary: Israel's corporate rejection of Messiah explains why the nation was not the immediate recipient of the new covenant's fulfillment, which awaits the millennial reign following the tribulation.

context: the gospel is the power of God unto salvation to the Jew first and then also to the Greek
point_summary: The gospel's saving power extends to Jewish people today on the same terms as Gentiles—through faith in Jesus Christ—while national Israel's ongoing rejection of Messiah does not nullify individual Jewish salvation.

context: as we look in Romans chapter 11, and I hope to package all this together for you this morning ... in verse number 16
point_summary: The holiness of the root (Israel's patriarchal covenant origins) extends to the branches, establishing why Gentile believers, grafted in, must not boast over broken-off Jewish branches.

context: Let me jump in in verse number 16 of Romans 11 It says for if the first fruit be holy the lump is also holy and if the root be holy So are the branches
point_summary: Ethnic descent alone confers no covenant standing; Israel's branches were broken off through unbelief, and Gentiles are grafted in only by faith, not by natural privilege.

context: Says in verse number 18 boasts not against the branches But if thou boasts thou bearest not the root but the root thee
point_summary: Gentile believers are warned against boasting over broken-off Jewish branches, since they stand only by faith grafted into a root that sustains them, not the reverse.

context: I'm looking verse number 20 well because of unbelief they Circle it underline it make sure you understand they were broken off
point_summary: Jewish branches were broken off the olive tree because of unbelief, while Gentile branches stand only by faith—a contrast demanding humility, not boasting.

context: And so we find that it continues in verse number 20 and it says and thou Standest by faith.
point_summary: Unbelief broke off the natural branches (Israel), while Gentiles stand by faith — a contrast demanding humility, not pride, before the God who can cut off or graft in according to His sovereign purposes.

context: Verse 21 for if God spared not the natural branches Take heed lest he also spare not thee
point_summary: God's sparing of natural branches (Israel) serves as a solemn warning to Gentile believers against pride, since the same unbelief that broke off Israel could result in their own removal.

context: Verse 22 behold Therefore the goodness and severity of God on them which fell severity, but toward thee goodness if thou continue in his goodness, otherwise thou also shall be cut off
point_summary: Romans 11:22 presents God's character as holding both goodness and severity in tension, warning Gentile believers against pride by showing that continued standing depends on remaining in faith.

context: And they also if they abide not still in unbelief shall be graft in for God is able to graft them in again
point_summary: God's ability to graft unbelieving Israel back in (v. 23) is presented as the divine provision by which Jewish people can be saved during the present church age.

context: And we find here that it continues in verse 24 for if thou were cut out of the olive tree, which is by
point_summary: Gentiles, grafted contrary to nature into the cultivated olive tree, serve as the basis for arguing that Israel's restoration to her own olive tree is even more certain.

context: lest he should be wise in your own conceits Notice this that blindness in part is happened to Israel until the fullness of the Gentiles become in
point_summary: Israel's partial spiritual blindness, lasting until the fullness of the Gentiles comes in, is a mystery God intends believers to understand rather than remain ignorant of, illustrated by the spiritually blind Jewish leaders in John 9.

reference_text: blindness in part is happened to Israel until the fullness of the Gentiles be come in
context: But we find here the Bible says that blindness in part is happened to Israel into the fullness of the Gentiles become in
point_summary: Israel's partial spiritual blindness, explained through Romans 11:25, is presented as the condition that opened the Church Age and the door of blessing for Gentiles until their fullness is complete.

reference_text: And so all Israel shall be saved: as it is written, There shall come out of Sion the Deliverer, and shall turn away ungodliness from Jacob
context: And it says in verse number 26 And so all Israel shall be saved as it is written there shall come out a sign to deliver and shall turn away Ungodliness from Jacob
point_summary: All Israel's salvation in Romans 11:26 is placed within a premillennial, dispensational timeline—fulfilled not universally at present, but at the Millennium following the Rapture and seven-year Tribulation.

reference_text: For this is my covenant unto them, when I shall take away their sins
context: for this is my covenant unto them when I shall take away their sins
point_summary: God's covenant to take away Israel's sins (v. 27) is identified as the new covenant, fulfilled not at the present age but at the Millennium following the Tribulation.

context: And so it's important that we understand the prophecy in Jeremiah, the truth in Romans chapter 11, as we take and unfold that which is given to us here in Hebrews chapter number 8.
point_summary: Romans 11 is held alongside Jeremiah 30–31 and Hebrews 8 to establish that the new covenant has a distinct, future fulfillment for Israel as a nation, particularly during and after the tribulation period.

context: Remember in Romans chapter 11, as we looked at the passage of Scripture, it spoke about the fact how we were graft in, and you understand that because of the rejection, because of their unbelief, then we that were wild, that we were from that wild olive tree, that we were graft in, and therefore we get to enjoy the blessings of salvation even today as individual believers.
point_summary: Gentiles, as wild branches grafted into the olive tree (Romans 11), share in the blessings of salvation that flow from Israel's covenant heritage, distinct from Israel as a nation.

context: I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ for it is the power of God unto salvation to everyone that believeth, to the Jew first and also to the Greek.
point_summary: The gospel's power unto salvation belongs entirely to God, not to the minister, making believers sufficient only as Spirit-filled vessels carrying what Paul called the power of God.

reference_text: the just shall live by faith
context: And we understand the quote from Habakkuk that is referenced not only here in the book of Hebrews, but also Paul uses it in Romans and again in Galatians, that the just shall live by faith.
point_summary: Romans 1:17's "the just shall live by faith" is cited as a cross-reference shared with Habakkuk and Galatians, establishing that living by faith is the consistent calling across Scripture and all of human history.

context: The Word of God tells us that we're all sinners, for all have sinned and come short of the glory of God.
point_summary: All human effort to earn salvation falls short, as Romans 3:23's declaration that all have sinned and come short of God's glory illustrates through Cain's rejected works-offering versus Abel's blood sacrifice.

context: The wages of sin is death. The gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ, our Lord.
point_summary: Salvation is a gift, not a wage — contrasted directly with the wages of sin being death — received by grace through faith in Christ's atoning work alone.

context: Being then made free from sin, you became the servants of righteousness.
point_summary: Romans 6:18 is brought in to show that freedom from sin's dominion does not mean freedom from servitude altogether — every person is either a servant of sin or a servant of righteousness.

context: but God be thanked that ye were the servants of sin, but ye have obeyed from the heart that form of doctrine which was delivered you.
point_summary: Freedom from sin's dominion comes through obeying "that form of doctrine"—the gospel—which transfers a believer from being sin's servant to being a servant of righteousness.

context: Being then made free from sin, you became the servants of righteousness.
point_summary: Freedom from sin's dominion, not freedom from all obligation, is the liberty Christ offers — every person serves either sin or righteousness, with the gospel transferring believers from one master to the other.

context: I'm not ashamed of the gospel of Christ, for it is the power of God unto salvation to everyone that believeth, to the Jew first and also to the Greek.
point_summary: Romans 1:16 is cited as Paul's own declaration of the gospel's power unto salvation, grounding the claim that obeying "that form of doctrine" liberates sinners from bondage to sin into righteousness.

context: but now being made free from sin and become servants to God, ye have your fruit unto holiness and the end everlasting life
point_summary: Freedom from sin through faith in the gospel results not in lawlessness but in a new servitude — becoming servants to God, bearing fruit unto holiness, and receiving everlasting life.

context: Aren't you thankful for the gift of God which is everlasting life, eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord
point_summary: Everlasting life, contrasted with sin's wages, is presented as a gift of God received through faith in the finished work of Christ—freedom from sin exchanged for servanthood to righteousness.

context: there is therefore now no condemnation to them which are in Christ Jesus who walk not after the flesh but after the spirit
point_summary: Freedom from condemnation belongs to those who are in Christ Jesus, with Romans 8:1 cited as confirmation that believers have been made free from the law of sin and death.

context: for the law of the spirit is life in Christ Jesus, notice, hath made me free from the law of sin and death
point_summary: The law of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus liberates believers from the law of sin and death, confirming the freedom from sin's bondage established throughout Romans 6.

context: wherefore God also gave them up to uncleanness through the lust of their own hearts to dishonor their own bodies between themselves
point_summary: God's abandonment of humanity to uncleanness (v. 24) illustrates the ongoing cultural battle in which people exchange the truth of God for a lie and worship the creature rather than the Creator.

context: who changed the truth of God into a lie, and worshiped and served the creature more than the Creator, who is blessed forever, amen
point_summary: Romans 1:25 is read as evidence that the ancient pattern of exchanging God's truth for a lie and worshiping the creature over the Creator is visibly repeating itself in contemporary society.

context: Really as you look at the entirety of this section of Romans chapter number 1, you find that they deny the existence of God because really they don't want to be accountable to God.
point_summary: Romans 1:24–25 grounds the sermon's claim that suppressing God's truth and exchanging it for a lie—seen in creature-worship over Creator-worship—reflects a timeless, society-wide battle for truth rooted in human unwillingness to be accountable to God.

context: For what sayeth the Scripture, Abraham believed God, and it was counted unto him for righteousness.
point_summary: Paul's citation of Genesis 15:6 in Romans 4:3 establishes that Abraham was counted righteous by faith well before circumcision was instituted, proving the sign did not produce his righteousness before God.

context: Look back in Romans 4 and look down at verse number 9, if you will
point_summary: Romans 4:9 is cited to establish that Abraham's righteousness was reckoned by faith while he was still uncircumcised, proving circumcision did not cause or contribute to his justification.

context: ...cometh this blessedness then upon the circumcision only, or upon the uncircumcision also? For we say that faith was reckoned to Abraham for righteousness.
point_summary: Romans 4:9 is used to establish that Abraham's faith was reckoned as righteousness before circumcision existed in his life, proving justification precedes and transcends the rite.

context: How was it then reckoned? When he was in circumcision or in uncircumcision? Not in circumcision, but in uncircumcision.
point_summary: The chronological priority of Genesis 15 over Genesis 17 proves that Abraham was justified by faith while still uncircumcised, establishing that righteousness is imputed apart from circumcision.

context: ...and he received the sign of circumcision, a seal of the righteousness of the faith which he had, yet being uncircumcised, that he might be the father of all them that believe, though they be not circumcised, that righteousness might be imputed unto them also.
point_summary: Circumcision is shown to be a sign and seal of a righteousness Abraham already possessed by faith before circumcision, establishing the chronological priority of Genesis 15 over Genesis 17.

context: the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord.
point_summary: Romans 6:23 is cited to anchor the concept of eternal redemption just established from Hebrews 9:12—contrasting sin's wage of death with God's gift of eternal life through Christ.

context: In fact, Romans 3 deals with that. We see how that we have this great truth and we see what he obtained for us, eternal redemption.
point_summary: Romans 3 is cited as scriptural support for the doctrine that Christ's once-for-all sacrifice secures remission of sin and eternal redemption that no human effort or religious observance can supplement.

context: I pray, God, that we'd offer up ourselves as a living sacrifice even as we see the truth in Romans 12, verses 1 and 2.
point_summary: Presenting oneself as a living sacrifice, as Romans 12:1–2 calls for, is framed here as the New Covenant counterpart to Old Testament priestly offerings, made possible by Christ's once-for-all atonement.

context: I pray, God, that we'd offer up ourselves as a living sacrifice even as we see the truth in Romans 12, verses 1 and 2.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: for all have sinned and come short of the glory of God.
point_summary: Denying personal sinfulness makes God a liar, since He has declared in Romans 3:23 that all have sinned and fallen short of His glory.

context: Turn to Romans chapter number 7. Look at this passage of Scripture because Paul testifies of himself here with regard to this truth of how sin is revealed to us through the Word of God.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: what shall we say then? Is the law sin? God forbid. Notice what he says, nay, I had not known sin, but by the law, for I had not known lust, except the law had said, thou shalt not covet
point_summary: The law's role as sin-revealer is cited to show that immersion in God's Word confronts believers with specific sins, making vague "if I have sinned" prayers inexcusable.

context: Notice what the Bible says in Romans chapter number 12
point_summary: Romans 12:1–2 is read together to show that presenting one's body as a living sacrifice and refusing conformity to the world are inseparable commands, the antidote to the worldly drift that destroyed Lot's family.

context: I beseech you therefore, brethren, by the mercies of God, that you present your bodies a living sacrifice, holy, acceptable unto God, which is your reasonable service
point_summary: Presenting one's body as a living sacrifice (v. 1) is paired inseparably with the command not to be conformed to the world (v. 2), with both verses together framing the answer to how believers avoid Lot's fatal drift toward worldly influence.

context: and be not conformed to this world, but be transformed by the renewing of your mind that you may prove what is that good and acceptable and perfect will of God
point_summary: Conformity to the world is resisted not by willpower but by the mind's transformation through immersion in Scripture, the only sufficient counter-influence to a culture celebrating wickedness.

context: Romans chapter number 1 is where we'll be for our study here this evening.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: I am debtor both to the Greeks and to the barbarians, both to the wise and to the unwise.
point_summary: Paul's description of himself as a "debtor" to all people frames gospel proclamation not merely as a responsibility but as a unique privilege belonging to every believer.

context: So as much as in me is, I am ready to preach the gospel to you that are at Rome also.
point_summary: Paul's readiness to preach the gospel at Rome illustrates that genuine love for the story of salvation must move beyond sentiment into active, willing proclamation.

context: For I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ, for it is the power of God unto salvation, to every one that believeth, to the Jew first and also to the Greek.
point_summary: The gospel of Christ is identified as the power of God unto salvation for all who believe, grounding the Christian's obligation and privilege to tell that story.

context: Paul says, notice what he says, I am debtor both to the Greeks and the barbarians, both to the wise and to the unwise.
point_summary: Paul's self-description as a debtor to all peoples is cited to reframe gospel proclamation not merely as duty but as a privilege God grants believers to bear good news.

context: Notice, back in Romans 1, Paul says this in v. 15, So, as much as in me is,
point_summary: Paul's declaration "I am ready" in verse 15 is taken as the biblical warrant for intentionally planning and prioritizing Gospel proclamation rather than leaving it to chance.

context: Back in Romans chapter number 1, Paul has said, I am debtor in verse 14.
point_summary: Paul's declaration "I am debtor" in Romans 1:14 frames gospel proclamation not as optional but as an obligatory priority, illustrated by the Apostle Paul's immediate, purposeful preaching upon entering Thessalonica.

context: He says in verse number 15, I am ready.
point_summary: Paul's declaration "I am ready" in verse 15 is presented as the second of three progressive commitments—debtor, ready, unashamed—framing gospel proclamation as a deliberate personal priority.

context: And in verse number 16 he says, for I am not ashamed. I'm not ashamed of the gospel of Christ. Why? For it, the gospel of Christ, is the power of God unto salvation to everyone that believeth, to the Jew first and also to the Greek.
point_summary: Paul's declaration "I am not ashamed" grounds the call to bold gospel witness in the gospel's own power—it is the power of God unto salvation—making shame before a silencing culture inexcusable.

context: It is the power of God unto salvation to everyone that believeth
point_summary: Paul's declaration "I am not ashamed" calls believers to resist cultural pressure to silence the gospel, grounded in the conviction that it is the power of God unto salvation for everyone who believes.

context: The book of Romans references the just shall live by faith.
point_summary: The Old Testament principle "the just shall live by faith" is cited in Romans 1:17 with its emphasis falling on justification—how the just are made right before God through faith.

reference_text: the wages of sin is death
context: we understand the Bible teaches us that the wages of sin is death
point_summary: Death's universal wages, grounded in Romans 6:23, are invoked to underscore that Abraham's physical death illustrates the biblical truth that sin brings death to all mankind.

context: Oftentimes, as I share the Gospel with individuals, I'll stop midstream of Romans 6 and verse 23, because I want to deal with the first portion of that Scripture.
point_summary: Sin's wages being death is only half the verse's message; the conjunction "but" pivots to the gift of eternal life through Christ, which the sermon uses to illustrate that God's forgiveness completes the story of human sinfulness.

context: The wages of sin is death... but the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord.
point_summary: Romans 6:23 is cited to show that sin's deadly wage is not the whole story — the conjunction "but" pivots to God's gift of eternal life, grounding the believer's confidence in divine forgiveness.

context: I said, hey, the Bible says for all have sinned and come short of the glory of God.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: the wages of sin is death, and that's what the Bible tells us in Romans chapter number 6
point_summary: The "judgments" category within the law—commandments broken, penalties incurred—finds its New Testament summation in Romans 6:23's declaration that the wages of sin is death, establishing universal guilt before God.

context: for the wages of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ our
point_summary: Salvation's terms — death as sin's wage and eternal life as God's gift through Christ — are grounded in Scripture, illustrating why the holy Word is able to make one wise unto salvation.

reference_text: fifth chapter of the book of Romans
context: You read the fifth chapter of the book of Romans and you'll find the impact of that disobedience of Adam upon all men
point_summary: Romans 5 is cited as the scriptural record of Adam's disobedience and its effect on all men, set against Christ's obedience and its saving impact on all who trust Him.

context: We live in a world where people do not know the peace of God. But our God, we understand we're able to make peace with God, according to Romans, through the Lord Jesus Christ.
point_summary: Peace with God through the Lord Jesus Christ, as referenced in Romans, is the prerequisite foundation from which the believer then experiences the surpassing peace of God promised to those who love His law.

context: if God be for us, who can be against us?
point_summary: God's omnipotence—declared and demonstrated throughout Scripture—means that whatever stands against the believer is ultimately overmatched, grounding the command to rejoice evermore.

context: The gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ, our Lord.
point_summary: God's valuation of human life is demonstrated in Christ's death to give eternal life—the "gift of God" of Romans 6:23 grounding the claim that we are valued by God.

context: therefore being justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ
point_summary: Justification by faith alone—not works—brings peace with God through Jesus Christ, whose sinless death and resurrection accomplished the redemption God planned before the foundation of the world.

context: but God commendeth his love toward us in that while we were yet sinners, Christ died for us.
point_summary: God's love for sinners is demonstrated not in our worthiness but in Christ dying for us while we were still his enemies, establishing our value before him.

context: but God commendeth his love toward us in that while we were yet sinners, Christ died for us.
point_summary: God's love demonstrated in Christ's death for sinners while they were still enemies establishes the believer's worth and value before God, grounding the truth that we are fearfully and wonderfully made.

context: His goal for us as his people is that we would be conformed to the image of his dear son.
point_summary: God's goal for his people—conformity to the image of his Son—is cited from Romans 8:29 as the divine purpose underlying trials and affliction.

context: faith cometh by hearing, and hearing by the word of God
point_summary: Faith comes by hearing the Word of God, making consistent engagement with Scripture the necessary means by which faith is increased and the believer is equipped to live by faith rather than by sight.

context: The Bible tells us that the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord.
point_summary: Eternal life is identified as God's gift through Jesus Christ, briefly cited as a blessing before the sermon moves on to expound abundant life from John 10:10.

context: Hold your place here and turn to the book of Romans, if you will, Romans chapter 12.
point_summary: Romans 12:17–19 is presented as a sister passage to 1 Thessalonians 5:15, reinforcing the same commands not to repay evil for evil and to pursue peace and non-retaliation toward all men.

context: ...in Romans 12, beginning in verse number 17, notice what the Bible says. Recompense to no man evil for evil...
point_summary: Romans 12:17's command to "recompense to no man evil for evil" is presented as a sister passage to 1 Thessalonians 5:15, together showing that overcoming evil with good is supernaturally unnatural and requires total dependence on the Lord.

context: ...Provide things honest in the sight of all men. If it be possible, as much as lieth in you, live peaceably with all men...
point_summary: Romans 12:18's call to live peaceably with all men is presented as a sister passage to 1 Thessalonians 5:15, together showing that overcoming evil with good is supernaturally demanding and requires total yieldedness to the Holy Spirit.

context: ...Notice 19, dearly beloved, avenge not yourselves, but rather give place unto wrath, for it is written, vengeance is mine, I will repay, saith the Lord...
point_summary: Surrendering personal vengeance is commanded because God alone holds the right to repay, making Romans 12:19 a direct parallel to the 1 Thessalonians 5:15 call to overcome evil with good.

context: ...Therefore, if thine enemy hunger, feed him. If he thirst, give him drink. For in so doing, thou shalt heap coals of fire on his head...
point_summary: Returning good to a hungry or thirsty enemy—heaping coals of fire on his head—is presented as supernaturally unnatural, requiring the Spirit's filling precisely because the flesh cannot fulfill it.

context: ...Verse 21, be not overcome of evil, but overcome evil with good...
point_summary: Romans 12:21's command to overcome evil with good is presented as supernaturally demanding—impossible for the flesh alone—revealing the believer's utter dependence on the Lord to obey it.

context: overcome evil with good
point_summary: Romans 12:21 supplies the sermon's title and governing command—"overcome evil with good"—which the preacher treats as the New Testament counterpart to the Proverbs 24–25 principle of refusing to repay evil in kind.

context: to see that we can overcome evil with good.
point_summary: Overcoming evil with good, as Paul commands in Romans 12:21, is illustrated by David's refusal to harm Saul, trusting God rather than acting in his own flesh.

context: ...We teach you, therefore, brethren, by the mercies of God, that you present your bodies a living sacrifice.
point_summary: Romans 12:1 is cited as one example of the spiritual sacrifices believers, as New Testament priests, are called to offer God—specifically the presentation of their bodies as a living sacrifice.

reference_text: Romans chapter five in verse number 10
context: If you read Romans chapter five in verse number 10, you understand that we're at enmity with God.
point_summary: Romans 5:10's reference to enmity with God grounds the sermon's first lesson: every human being, without exception, possesses a sin nature that places them in natural opposition to God before salvation.

context: Romans 5 in verse number 20, down through verse number two of chapter six
point_summary: Where sin abounded in Genesis 38, grace abounded even more, illustrating that God's gracious response to human wickedness surpasses the offense itself.

context: Romans 5 in verse number 20, down through verse number two of chapter six
point_summary: Believers, freed from sin's bondage by grace, are not licensed to continue in sin—a truth made sharper because, unlike the lost, they possess the Holy Spirit and know better.

context: Moreover the law entered that the offense might abound. Notice what the Bible says, but where sin abounded. I would say in Genesis 38, sin abounded. The Bible says, Grace did much more abound.
point_summary: Where sin abounded in Genesis 38, grace did much more abound—yet this superabounding grace is no license for continued sin, as God forbid answers that very presumption.

context: That as sin hath reigned unto death, even so might grace reign through righteousness unto eternal life by Jesus Christ our Lord.
point_summary: Where sin abounded in Genesis 38, grace abounded even more, yet this reality of God's gracious pardon does not promote continued sinful living among His people.

context: Verse one of chapter six, what shall we say then? Shall we continue in sin that grace may abound?
point_summary: Romans 6:1's rhetorical question is cited to preempt the misuse of divine grace as license for sin, affirming that God's readiness to forgive does not promote continued sinful living.

context: Notice very pointedly in verse number two, God forbid. How shall we that are dead to sin live any longer therein?
point_summary: God's abounding grace toward sinners, illustrated in Genesis 38, does not license continued sin, since those dead to sin cannot rightly go on living in it.

context: Notice in verse 17 of chapter six, it says, But God be thanked that ye were the servants of sin, but ye have obeyed from the heart that form of doctrine which was delivered to you.
point_summary: Romans 6:17 is cited as evidence that believers have been freed from slavery to sin and made servants of righteousness, grounding the claim that God's grace does not license continued sinful living.

context: Being then made free from sin, ye became the servants of righteousness.
point_summary: Freedom from sin's slavery—becoming a servant of righteousness rather than sin—is cited as evidence of God's grace, which motivates holy living rather than license to sin.

context: Romans 9 talks about it, and you can study the Scriptures...
point_summary: Romans 9 is cited to confirm the contextual, national-election meaning of "the elder shall serve the younger" before the sermon moves to its applicational layer about the old and new natures.

context: In Romans chapter number 6, we'll jump in here in just a moment, but I want you to understand the Christian life is a journey.
point_summary: Romans 6 is introduced as the evening's primary text for understanding the Christian life as a Spirit-led journey in which the flesh, though not eradicated at salvation, must be actively resisted.

context: Romans chapter number 6, we're going to look at verses 1 through 19 this evening.
point_summary: Romans 6:1 introduces a passage where the defining challenge of the Christian life is framed as learning to yield—to submit and give way—since such surrender is contrary to natural human inclination.

context: Romans chapter number 6, we're going to look at verses 1 through 19 this evening.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: we look here in Romans chapter 6 and we see the use of this word, we may not fully understand what it means to yield. Let me ask this question, is it a natural thing to yield? It's not natural. It's not natural. The word yield means to submit, to give way, or to give place. That's what it means. Now we're instructed in the text of Scripture, I've titled the message this evening from verse number 13, that we're to yield ourselves unto God.
point_summary: Yielding to God—defined here as submitting and giving way—runs contrary both to the flesh and to a culture that tells people to do whatever pleases them.

context: Romans chapter number 6, and we find this truth that we are to yield ourselves unto God.
point_summary: Yielding to God runs contrary to both fallen human nature and a culture that tells individuals to follow their own desires, making the command of Romans 6 countercultural and spiritually demanding.

context: what shall we say then? Shall we continue in sin that grace may abound?
point_summary: Romans 6:1's rhetorical question — whether believers should continue in sin so grace may increase — is answered against the backdrop of a culture that tells people to do whatever pleases them, contrasting that impulse with the call to yield to Christ as Lord.

context: moreover the law entered that the offense might abound, but where sin abounded, grace did much more abound
point_summary: Romans 5:20's declaration that where sin abounded grace did much more abound sets up the antinomian misuse of grace that Romans 6 directly refutes.

context: that as sin hath reigned unto death, even so might grace reign through righteousness unto eternal life by Jesus Christ our Lord
point_summary: Grace's reign through righteousness unto eternal life (5:21) provides the backdrop against which the antinomian misuse of grace—doing as one pleases because grace abounds—is exposed and refuted in Romans 6.

context: what shall we say then? Shall we continue in sin that grace may abound?
point_summary: Romans 6:1 poses the antinomian question—shall believers sin freely since grace abounds?—which the sermon answers by contrasting true grace, which teaches godly living, against the false teachers' distortion of grace into licentiousness.

context: God forbid
point_summary: The "God forbid" answer to whether believers should continue in sin exposes the false teaching that grace permits unbridled lust, contrasted with Titus 2's picture of grace that teaches godly living.

context: where sin did abound, that grace did abound much more
point_summary: Grace's superabundance over sin, celebrated in Romans 5:20, sets up Paul's counter to the antinomian misuse of that truth—that abounding grace is no license to continue in sin.

context: we don't continue in sin that grace may abound
point_summary: Romans 6:1 raises the antinomian misuse of grace—the false conclusion that abounding grace licenses continued sin—which Paul immediately rejects as a fundamental misreading of what grace actually produces.

context: how shall we that are dead to sin live any longer therein?
point_summary: The "God forbid" of verse 2 answers the antinomian misuse of grace by grounding the impossibility of continuing in sin in the believer's death to sin and new position in Christ.

context: he says in verse 3
point_summary: Baptism into Christ's death and resurrection establishes the believer's new position—dead to sin and obligated to walk in newness of life, not to continue in sin under grace.

context: We are buried with him by baptism into death, that like as Christ was raised up from the dead by the glory of the Father, even so we also should walk in newness of life.
point_summary: Baptism pictures the believer's union with Christ in death and resurrection, signifying that those in Christ are raised to walk in newness of life as a new creature.

context: for if we have been planted together in the likeness of his death, we shall be also in the likeness of his resurrection
point_summary: Union with Christ in his death guarantees union with his resurrection, establishing the believer's new life as inseparable from Christ's own victory over death.

context: knowing this, that our old man is crucified with him, that the body of sin might be destroyed, that henceforth we should not serve sin
point_summary: Believers are meant to *know* that the old man's crucifixion with Christ was purposeful—to destroy the body of sin so that sin no longer holds them as master.

context: for he that is dead is freed from sin
point_summary: Death to sin brings freedom from its power, as the one who has died with Christ is declared freed from sin's dominion—no longer obligated to serve it.

context: Now, if we be dead with Christ, we believe that we shall also live with him
point_summary: Death to sin through union with Christ's death carries the inseparable promise of resurrection life with him, grounded in the fact that Christ, having conquered death, dies no more.

context: now if we be dead with Christ, we believe that we shall also live with him
point_summary: Death with Christ is inseparable from resurrection life with him, so that the believer who has died to sin is also alive unto God—a truth the sermon connects to Galatians 2:20 as its one-verse summary.

context: knowing that Christ being raised from the dead dieth no more, death hath no more dominion over him
point_summary: Christ's resurrection being permanent and unrepeatable — death having no more dominion over him — grounds the believer's confidence that union with him means sharing in a life that cannot be undone.

context: for in that he died, he died unto sin, what does it say? Once
point_summary: Christ's death for sin was a singular, completed sacrifice — offered "once" — grounding the believer's position as dead to sin and alive to God.

context: He says this in verse 11, likewise reckon ye also yourselves to be dead indeed unto sin, but alive unto God through Jesus Christ our Lord.
point_summary: Believers are called to reckon themselves dead to sin and alive unto God—recognizing their position in Christ as the basis for the practical counsel to yield themselves to God rather than to sin.

context: Let not sin therefore reign in your mortal body that you should obey it in the lust thereof.
point_summary: Believers are commanded not to let sin reign in their mortal body because, through union with Christ, they have been delivered from sin's dominion and are now alive unto God.

context: And here's the counsel, verse 13, neither yield ye your members as instruments of unrighteousness unto sin, but yield yourselves unto God as those that are alive from the dead and your members as instruments of righteousness unto God,
point_summary: Believers are counseled in verse 13 to stop yielding their members as instruments of unrighteousness and instead yield themselves to God, because in Christ they have been delivered from sin's dominion and power.

context: for sin shall not have dominion over you, for you're not under the law, but under grace.
point_summary: Grace frees the believer from sin's dominion, so that whereas the lost are bound under sin's power, those in Christ are dead to sin and alive unto God.

context: for sin shall not have dominion over you
point_summary: Salvation in Christ delivers the believer not only from sin's penalty (death) but from sin's power, so that sin shall not have dominion over those who are alive unto God.

context: neither yield ye your members
point_summary: Believers are called to yield themselves and their members to God as instruments of righteousness, because in Christ they have been delivered from sin's penalty and power, though not yet its presence.

context: Shall we sin because we are not under the law, but under grace? God forbid.
point_summary: Grace's freedom from law does not eliminate the believer's ongoing choice of whom to obey, making yielding to God a recurring decision rather than a one-time event.

context: Know ye not that to whom ye yield yourselves servants to obey, his servants ye are to whom ye obey, whether of sin unto death, or of obedience unto righteousness.
point_summary: Yielding to God is not a one-time decision but a recurring choice every believer faces, determining whether one serves sin unto death or obedience unto righteousness.

context: If you read Romans chapter eight in verse number 29, he predestined us to be conformed to the image of his dear son.
point_summary: Salvation's purpose exceeds escaping hell; citing Romans 8:29, the sermon declares God predestined believers to be conformed to the image of His Son, not to continue living for themselves.

context: being then made free from sin, you became the servants of righteousness
point_summary: Freedom from sin's mastery means the believer has exchanged one servitude for another—now a servant of righteousness rather than sin—a contrast the sermon presents as cause for gratitude.

context: I speak after the manner of men
point_summary: Just as believers once yielded their members as servants to uncleanness and iniquity, they are now called to yield those same members as servants to righteousness unto holiness.

context: For as you have yielded your member servants to uncleanness and to iniquity unto iniquity, even so now yield your member servants to righteousness unto holiness.
point_summary: Paul's parallel in Romans 6:19 calls believers to redirect the same yielding they once gave to uncleanness and iniquity, now giving that same surrender to righteousness unto holiness.

context: Neither yield ye your members as instruments of unrighteousness unto sin.
point_summary: Salvation does not make righteous living automatic; believers can still yield their members to unrighteousness, making the command to yield to God a necessary, ongoing choice.

context: But we see in a real sense in Romans chapter six, yield yourselves unto God.
point_summary: Yielding oneself to God, as Romans 6 commands, is a daily, repeated choice that brings glory to God rather than deference to self or others.

context: For the Bible says that God commendeth his love toward us, and that while we were yet sinners, Christ died for us in Romans five and verse number eight.
point_summary: God's love was not merely declared but demonstrated in action, as Christ's death for sinners in Romans 5:8 is cited to ground the believer's position as a child of God in 1 John 3:1.

context: but where sin abounded, grace did much more abound
point_summary: Grace abounding over sin (Rom. 5:20) is cited to show how false teachers twist that truth into a license for sin, which Paul immediately refutes in Romans 6:1–2.

context: what shall we say then? Shall we continue in sin that grace
point_summary: Grace is never a license to continue in sin, as Paul's rhetorical question and emphatic denial in Romans 6:1 directly refute the false teaching that God's grace makes obedience irrelevant.

context: Paul says this, If it had not been for the law, I would not have known sin, in Romans chapter number 7.
point_summary: Paul's statement in Romans 7 is cited to show that the law's function is to reveal and expose sin, making the transgressor aware of his lawlessness before God.

context: for all have sinned and come short of the glory of God
point_summary: Sin is affirmed as a universal human condition—every person without exception has sinned and fallen short of God's glory—making Christ's atoning work the only remedy.

context: Do you remember what Paul said in Romans chapter number 7? He said, the things that I would, I do not.
point_summary: Romans 7:15 illustrates that self-effort in sanctification produces only inner conflict, pointing to the need for yielding to Christ rather than striving in one's own flesh.

context: as he says there in Romans 6, 7, and 8
point_summary: Romans 6–8 is cited as Paul's extended treatment of sanctification, where yielding one's members to God—not self-effort—is the mechanism by which Christ works holiness in the believer.

context: as he says there in Romans 6, 7, and 8
point_summary: Romans 7's confession that "the things I would, I do not" is cited to show that sanctification, like salvation, cannot be achieved by human effort but only through Christ's delivering work.

context: as he says there in Romans 6, 7, and 8
point_summary: Romans 6–8 is cited as Paul's extended treatment of sanctification, establishing that the process is not achieved by human striving in the flesh but by yielding to the work of Christ and the Holy Spirit.

context: the things that I would, I do not. And the things that I would not, that I do.
point_summary: The frustration of moral self-effort—echoing Paul's confession that the good he would do he does not—underscores why sanctification requires abiding in Christ rather than striving in one's own strength.

context: ...the testimony of the Apostle Paul. There are three statements that he makes in Romans chapter number 1 verse 14...
point_summary: Paul's readiness to preach the gospel, cited in Romans 1:15, is used to parallel Ezra's prepared heart—illustrating that spiritual readiness precedes and enables the opportunity to minister God's Word.

context: ...the testimony of the Apostle Paul. There are three statements that he makes in Romans chapter number 1 verse 14, 15...
point_summary: Readiness to preach the gospel, modeled by Paul's declaration in Romans 1:15, is held up as the New Testament parallel to Ezra's prepared-heart posture as a ready scribe.

context: ...the testimony of the Apostle Paul. There are three statements that he makes in Romans chapter number 1 verse 14, 15 and 16...
point_summary: The readiness Paul declares in Romans 1:15 — "I am ready to preach the gospel" — is held up as a New Testament parallel to Ezra's prepared-heart readiness to teach God's Word.

context: so as much as in me is I am ready to preach the gospel to you that are at Rome also
point_summary: Paul's declaration "I am ready to preach the gospel" in Romans 1:15 is held up as a New Testament parallel to Ezra's preparedness — readiness precedes opportunity.

context: professing themselves to be wise, they became fools
point_summary: Professing worldly wisdom while rejecting God's truth produces foolishness, a pattern the sermon identifies as characteristic of false teachers and the present age.

context: I believe that Romans 12 gives us great truth with regard to this response of ours, as we would examine and acknowledge, and perhaps there's one that would not be too proud to acknowledge before God. God already knows that if you've been beguiled. He's just trying to help you understand that you've been beguiled. He already knows. He's trying to help you to know that you've been beguiled, and on your way to being spoiled. And if you would humble yourself and acknowledge that, and let God help you. Romans 12, we're familiar with verse number one,
point_summary: Immersing oneself in Scripture renews the mind and guards against conformity to the world's philosophies, serving as the corrective response to having been beguiled by false teaching.

context: I beseech you therefore, brethren, by the mercies of God, that you present your bodies a living sacrifice, holy, acceptable unto God, which is your reasonable service.
point_summary: Romans 12:1–2 is invoked as the antidote to being "beguiled," calling believers to present themselves to God and renew their minds through Scripture rather than conforming to worldly teaching.

context: And be not conformed to this world... be transformed by the renewing of your mind.
point_summary: Renewing the mind through Scripture—not conforming to the world's flow—is presented as the believer's defense against false teaching that permeates society.

context: Romans 12 and verse number 11, the Bible says this, in this portion of Scripture, when we think about the responsibility, it says in the workplace, not slothful in business, fervent in spirit, serving the Lord.
point_summary: Working "as to the Lord" rather than for human approval makes slothfulness in any task impossible, because the worker's true audience is God himself.

context: The Bible says, so then faith cometh by hearing and hearing by the word of God.
point_summary: Faith grows through hearing God's word, and the sermon cites Romans 10:17 as the scriptural basis for the 2017 theme of increasing faith.

context: Just turn to the book of Romans, if you will. In Romans chapter number eight, great picture, I believe, of resting in God's love for us.
point_summary: Romans 8 is presented as a "great picture" of resting in God's love amid real storms and anxiety, grounded in the assurance that God's love, power, and wisdom are working for our good.

context: what shall we then say to these things? If God be for us, who can be against us?
point_summary: Because God is for us and gave his own Son for us, believers can rest assured in his love even when storms, difficulties, and anxiety press hard against them.

context: He that spared not his own son, but delivered him up for us all, how shall he not with him also freely give us all things?
point_summary: God's willingness to deliver up his own Son for us all is presented as the supreme, implicit proof of his love — a love in which believers are called to rest amid real storms and anxiety.

context: who shall lay anything to the charge of God's elect? It is God that justifieth
point_summary: God's justification of the elect silences every accusation against them, grounding the believer's assurance that nothing — tribulation, distress, persecution — can separate them from Christ's love.

context: who is he that condemneth? It is Christ that died, yea, rather, that is risen again
point_summary: Christ's present intercession at the right hand of God—cited in verse 34—anchors the believer's assurance that nothing can condemn or separate them from God's love amid life's storms.

context: who is even at the right hand of God, who also maketh intercession for us.
point_summary: Christ's present intercession at the right hand of God, alongside his death and resurrection, stands as the believer's assurance that no condemnation can stand and no circumstance can sever them from God's love.

context: Who shall separate us from the love of Christ? Shall tribulation, or distress, or persecution, or famine, or nakedness, or peril, or sword?
point_summary: No force in creation — tribulation, death, height, depth, or any other — can sever the believer from the love of Christ, making Romans 8:35–39 a "recipe for resting in God's love."

context: As it is written, for thy sake we are killed all the day long, we are counted as sheep for the slaughter,
point_summary: Even the most extreme sufferings—being killed all day long, counted as sheep for slaughter—cannot separate believers from the love of Christ, making Romans 8 a "recipe for resting in God's love."

context: nay, in all these things. We are more than conquerors through him that loved us.
point_summary: Even amid tribulation, persecution, and every conceivable threat, believers are more than conquerors through Christ's love—a truth the sermon presents as the foundation for resting in God's love.

context: For I am persuaded. I'm thankful for that word, that phrase that Paul uses. He says that neither death, nor life, nor angels, nor principalities, nor powers, nor things present, nor things to come,
point_summary: No created thing, no circumstance, and no power in existence can sever the believer from God's love in Christ — a certainty Paul's persuasion grounds as the very basis for resting in that love.

context: nor height, nor depth, nor any other creature shall be able to separate us from the love of God, which is in Christ Jesus our Lord.
point_summary: No created thing—death, life, angels, powers, height, or depth—can sever the believer from God's love in Christ, and that exhaustive list is presented as the very ground of rest in God's love.

context: but he goes through all these things that are not going to be able to separate us, but then just in case somebody would come along and try to fit something in, he says, nor any other creature. Listen, there's nothing that's going to separate you from the love of God.
point_summary: The exhaustive catalog of Romans 8:39—closing with "nor any other creature"—is presented as a deliberate, all-encompassing guarantee that nothing can separate the believer from God's love, making it a settled foundation for rest.

context: and we know that all things work together for what? For good! To them that love God, to them who are the called according to His purpose
point_summary: God's goodness is not contingent on circumstances, and Romans 8:28's promise that all things work together for good grounds the call to rest in His love regardless of present disorder.

context: let us, therefore, follow after the things which make for peace, and things wherewith one may edify another.
point_summary: Edification — actively building others up — is presented as the primary means of fulfilling the command to support the weak, with Romans 14:19 supplying the direct Pauline warrant for pursuing peace and mutual upbuilding.

context: we then that are strong ought to bear the infirmities of the weak and not to please ourselves.
point_summary: Strong believers are obligated to bear the infirmities of the weak rather than please themselves, framing edification as a selfless act of support rather than personal gratification.

context: the gift of God, which is eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord.
point_summary: Salvation cannot be earned or worked for, but is received as God's gift of eternal life through Jesus Christ, because sinful humanity stands separated from God and unable to attain redemption on its own.

context: The Bible says, as you study the pages of Scripture, that the gift of God is eternal life, but prior to that it says the wages of sin is death.
point_summary: Salvation is framed as a stark contrast between what sin earns—eternal separation from God—and what God freely gives, with the wage of death being what every sinner justly deserves.

context: wages of sin is death. There's a great contrast between a wage and a gift. What I earn for my sin is death. Separation from God for all of eternity. That's what I deserve. That's what you deserve for your sin. That is the just judgment of God for your sin and my sin. But God in his mercy and his grace, he says, but I offer you a gift and the gift that I offer you is eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord.
point_summary: Sin earns death—eternal separation from God—while eternal life is a contrasting gift of grace through Jesus Christ, not anything humanity could earn or deserve.

context: 'For whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved.'
point_summary: The universal scope of salvation — "whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved" — is cited to affirm that no heritage, lineage, or nationality excludes anyone from receiving God's grace.

context: The Bible says the wages of sin is death. But the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord.
point_summary: Sin's universal penalty — death — and its remedy — eternal life through Jesus Christ — ground the "whosoever" offer of remission of sins proclaimed in Peter's gospel preaching to the Gentiles.

context: For all have sinned and come short of the glory of God.
point_summary: Universal sinfulness—"all have sinned and come short of the glory of God"—is cited to refute the waitress's assumption that personal goodness determines one's standing before God.

context: For all have sinned and come short of the glory of God.
point_summary: Romans 3:23 is cited to affirm universal sinfulness as the shared human condition that makes salvation through Christ alone — not personal merit or works — the only basis for anyone's redemption.

context: And she had taken a red pen and a ruler and underlined Romans chapter 10, verses 9 and 10 and 13.
point_summary: Confession of Christ's lordship and belief in his resurrection, as Romans 10:9 plainly states, were the very words that brought the speaker under saving conviction and to personal faith.

context: And she had taken a red pen and a ruler and underlined Romans chapter 10, verses 9 and 10 and 13.
point_summary: Belief in the heart and confession with the mouth, as underlined in Romans 10:9–10 and 13, were the precise gospel terms that brought the preacher himself to saving faith.

context: And she had taken a red pen and a ruler and underlined Romans chapter 10, verses 9 and 10 and 13.
point_summary: Salvation through calling on the name of the Lord is presented as the decisive moment of personal reckoning, where intellectual assent became conscious confession.

context: And I opened that Bible to Romans chapter 10, verses 9 and 10 and 13, and read them.
point_summary: Confession of Christ's lordship and belief in his resurrection, as stated in Romans 10:9, were the convicting truths the preacher recognized as already true in his heart before he knelt and called on God for salvation.

context: And I opened that Bible to Romans chapter 10, verses 9 and 10 and 13, and read them.
point_summary: Belief in the heart and confession with the mouth converged in a personal moment of conviction, as Romans 10:10 became the scriptural ground on which saving faith was exercised.

context: And I came to Romans chapter 13, I'm sorry, chapter 10 and verse 13, for whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved.
point_summary: Salvation's universal promise in Romans 10:13 proved personally decisive—reading "whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved" moved the preacher to kneel and cry out to God, who answered faithfully.

context: the Apostle Paul says that he had not known sin but by the law in Romans chapter number 7 he says for I had not known lust except the law had said thou shalt not covet
point_summary: The law's function, illustrated from Romans 7:7, is to expose sin and drive sinners to recognize their need of Christ as Savior.

context: but God commended his love toward us in that while we were yet sinners, Christ died for us.
point_summary: Christ's death was not merely exemplary but redemptive—the specific payment required by God's plan to save sinners, as confirmed by Romans 5:8's declaration that he died for us while we were yet sinners.

context: and we spoke about, in Romans chapter 11, how that we've been graft in,
point_summary: Romans 11's teaching on Gentile believers being grafted in is invoked to show that the new covenant's promise of sins remembered no more extends beyond Israel to individual believers today.

context: For whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved.
point_summary: Salvation requires no human achievement because Christ's finished work on the cross is complete, and "whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord" receives that gift freely.

context: The Bible says there's none good. No, not one.
point_summary: No human work merits salvation because Scripture declares none righteous, grounding the call to trust Christ's atoning work rather than personal effort.

context: For all have sinned and come short in the glory of God.
point_summary: Universal sinfulness establishes that no one can earn salvation by works, making Christ's atoning remission the only basis for pardon.

context: As for by one man, sin entered into the world, Romans chapter 5 and verse number 12.
point_summary: Despite Eve being the one deceived, Romans 5:12 is cited to show that Adam bears sole federal responsibility for sin's entrance into the world.

context: for we know that all things work together for good
point_summary: Romans 8:28 is invoked as lived reality rather than theory—suffering is real (as with Joseph's pit and prison), yet God works all things together for good, ultimately bringing his people to the palace.

context: for whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved
point_summary: Romans 10:13 functions as one of two bookend promises framing the passage, anchoring the universal offer of salvation to all who call on the Lord before the sermon traces the missionary chain required to make that calling possible.

context: How then shall they call on Him in whom they have not believed? And how shall they believe in Him of whom they have not heard? And how shall they hear without a preacher?
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: And how shall they preach except they be sent? As it is written, how beautiful are the feet of them that preach the gospel of peace and bring glad tidings of good things.
point_summary: Sent preachers with beautiful feet are the necessary human link in the chain from God's saving purpose to the world hearing the gospel, framing the entire passage as a missions text.

context: But they have not all obeyed the gospel. For Isaiah saith, Lord, who hath believed our report?
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: So then faith cometh by hearing, and hearing by the word of God.
point_summary: Romans 10:17 functions as the closing bookend of the passage, paired with verse 13, framing the entire missionary chain—from sending to preaching to hearing to faith—as two promises bracketing the call to global missions.

context: How Shall They Preach Except They Be Sent?
point_summary: Verse 15 supplies the sermon's title and frames the passage's central concern: that gospel proclamation requires prior sending, a missionary-chain logic Paul traces through the surrounding questions.

context: for whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved
point_summary: Romans 10:13's universal scope—"whosoever"—means any living person who calls on the Lord's name is guaranteed salvation, the word "shall" expressing certainty rather than mere possibility.

context: whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved
point_summary: Romans 10:13's universal scope—"whosoever"—means salvation is available to any living person, and the word "shall" makes that promise certain rather than merely possible.

context: The Bible says, so then faith cometh by hearing, and hearing by the word of God.
point_summary: Faith grows through engagement with Scripture, because Romans 10:17 directly answers the complaint of having little faith by pointing to the Word of God as the source from which faith comes.

context: But the reality is that these two precious promises, verse 13, for whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved.
point_summary: Salvation by calling on the Lord's name (v. 13) and faith coming through the Word (v. 17) are presented as bookend promises framing the intervening questions about gospel proclamation.

context: Verse 17, so then faith cometh by hearing and hearing by the Word of God.
