point_summary: Faith comes by hearing the Word of God, and this verse is paired with verse 13 as a "bookend promise" framing the passage's urgent questions about preaching and sending.

context: It says in verse number 14, how then shall they call on Him in whom they have not believed? But then it says this, and how shall they believe in Him of whom they have not heard? And how shall they hear without a preacher?
point_summary: The three rhetorical questions of Romans 10:14 form a logical chain showing that calling on Christ presupposes belief, belief presupposes hearing, and hearing presupposes a preacher—making proclamation indispensable to salvation.

context: for whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved
point_summary: Salvation's universal promise — "whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved" — is rendered tragically unreachable for millions who have never heard, making missionary sending an urgent necessity.

context: and how shall they preach except they be sent?
point_summary: Effective gospel proclamation requires a sender, as the chain from calling to believing to hearing to preaching necessarily begins with someone being commissioned and dispatched to the field.

context: so then faith cometh by hearing, and hearing by the Word of God
point_summary: Faith to believe the gospel comes only through hearing, and hearing comes only through the preached Word—making the missionary's sent proclamation the indispensable link in the chain to salvation.

context: how shall they believe in Him of whom they have not heard?
point_summary: Hearing the gospel through a sent preacher is the necessary precondition for belief, so that those previously blinded by false religion and culture can now hear and exercise saving faith.

context: how are they gonna preach except they be sent?
point_summary: Sending churches that give and pray are indispensable links in the chain by which people hear, believe, and call—making their missionary support "fruit" credited to their own account.

context: Romans chapter number 1, the Bible begins, the Apostle Paul is writing to those that are in Rome. Notice what he says, Paul, a servant of Jesus Christ.
point_summary: Paul's self-identification as one "separated unto the gospel of God" frames the resurrection message as the very gospel to which apostolic ministry is wholly consecrated.

context: Look at verse number 2, which he had promised afore by his prophets, notice, in the holy Scriptures.
point_summary: The resurrection of Christ was no afterthought but was promised beforehand by God's prophets in the holy Scriptures, grounding the Gospel Paul preached in prophetic fulfillment.

context: notice verse 4, and declared to be the Son of God with power according to the spirit of holiness by the resurrection from the dead,
point_summary: Christ's resurrection is presented as the divine declaration that Jesus is the Son of God with power, grounding the Gospel message that Paul carried to all nations.

context: I am not ashamed of the Gospel of Christ, for it is the power of God unto salvation to every one that believeth. Aren't you thankful for that? To the Jew first and also to the Greek.
point_summary: The resurrection of Christ, previewed in Romans 1:4, grounds Paul's unashamed declaration in Romans 1:16 that the Gospel is God's power unto salvation for all nations.

context: We find even as we read the portion of Scripture in Romans chapter number one, that even the Old Testament prophecies prophesied not only of the birth of Christ, but of His death and of His resurrection.
point_summary: Old Testament prophecy, the sermon notes from Romans 1, foretold not only Christ's birth but also His death and resurrection, confirming that God always fulfills what He declares.

context: As the book of Romans tells us, how shall they hear without a preacher?
point_summary: The proclamation of the risen Christ depends on obedient messengers, since others cannot hear without someone willing to tell them—just as the disciples needed Mary Magdalene's report.

context: Paul says, I am not ashamed. We heard the kids last week on Sunday morning do such a wonderful job of quoting the verse in Romans 1.16. For I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ. Why? For it is the power of God unto salvation to everyone that believeth. To the Jew first and also to the Greek.
point_summary: God's will for the church age is the worldwide proclamation of the gospel, as Paul declares in Romans 1:16 that the gospel is God's power unto salvation for all who believe.

context: whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved
point_summary: Calling upon the Lord in saving faith—available to "whosoever"—is presented as the open door for any unbeliever within hearing to receive Christ that very moment.

context: 'What advantage then hath the Jew, or what profit is there of circumcision?'
point_summary: God's entrustment of His oracles to the Jewish people (v. 1–2) is used to ground the argument that biblical preservation operated through a divinely appointed human instrument, namely the scribal tradition.

context: 'Much every way, chiefly because that unto them were committed the oracles of God.'
point_summary: God's entrustment of His oracles to the Jewish people illustrates how He worked through human instruments—specifically the scribes—as the means of preserving and transmitting His Word.

context: So then faith cometh by hearing and hearing by the Word of the Lord or the Word of God
point_summary: Daily, consistent time in God's Word builds the spiritual preparation needed so that when crisis, trial, or temptation arrives, faith—which comes by hearing that Word—is already at work.

context: the Gospel is the power of God unto salvation
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: The book of Romans tells us this in Romans chapter number eight, that we are to be conformed to the image of his dear son.
point_summary: Conformity to the image of Christ, cited from Romans 8, is presented as one of the evidences of genuine conversion that should mark the life of a born-again believer.

context: we are overcomers. We are more than conquerors, Romans 8 and verse number 37 tells us.
point_summary: Because Christ triumphed over principalities and powers, believers share in that victory and are declared more than conquerors—not defeated, but overcoming.

context: we are more than conquerors through Him that loved us.
point_summary: Because Christ destroyed the devil through His death, believers are declared "more than conquerors" and are called to live in victory rather than defeat.

context: The gospel is the power of God unto salvation
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Paul says this, I know that in me that is in my flesh dwelleth no good thing.
point_summary: Paul's confession that no good thing dwells in his flesh grounds the sermon's point that the believer's victory comes not from self but from Christ.

context: All have sin and come short of the glory of God
point_summary: All humanity stands as sinners falling short of God's glory, making Christ's atoning death on the cross a necessary payment for sin.

context: For the wages of sin is death.
point_summary: The wages of sin is death, but God's mercy and grace provide salvation that sinners cannot earn — a transaction available to anyone who believes.

context: God commendeth His love toward us in that while we're yet sinners, Christ died for us.
point_summary: God's love demonstrated in Christ's substitutionary death for sinners who have nothing to offer is the ground of salvation received entirely by grace, not merit.

context: The Bible says, for whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: the promise of Romans 10, 13 is as true today as it ever has been, for whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved
point_summary: The universal promise of Romans 10:13 — that whosoever calls on the Lord's name shall be saved — is cited as the unchanging basis for confidence that souls can be saved in the upcoming outreach.

context: Well, why? Because all sin comes short of the glory of God.
point_summary: Universal sinfulness establishes why grace must appear to all men, grounding the call to holy, separated living in the reality that none are righteous before God.

context: There's none righteous, no, not one.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: faith cometh by hearing, and hearing by the word of God
point_summary: Faith's growth is tied directly to the Word of God, as Romans 10:17 is cited to explain why the Thessalonians' faith had grown exceedingly between Paul's two letters.

context: Paul said, I know that in me that is in my flesh dwelleth no good thing, right? He says, the things that I would, I do not. The things that I would not, that I do.
point_summary: Romans 7:18 is invoked to establish that the flesh has no capacity for godly living, making Spirit-empowered surrender the only viable means of obeying God's household instructions.

context: and that knowing the time, that now it is high time, listen, to awake out of sleep. For now is our salvation nearer than when we believed.
point_summary: Spiritual urgency—that it is "high time to awake out of sleep"—frames the call to revival, renewed obedience, and casting off darkness in preparation for walking honestly before God.

context: The night is far spent, the day is at hand. Let us therefore cast off the works of darkness and let us put on the armor of light.
point_summary: Spiritual urgency demands that believers cast off sin's darkness and clothe themselves in Christ, awakening to the nearness of salvation before the night fully passes.

context: Let us walk honestly.
point_summary: Genuine revival calls believers to walk honestly—renouncing the works of darkness—as a mark of awakened, obedient Christian living in the present hour.

context: Let us walk honestly, as in the day, not in rioting and in drunkenness, not in chambering and wantonness, not in strife and envying
point_summary: Walking honestly and refusing to make provision for the flesh are presented as the defining marks of the believer's daily conduct in a sin-cursed world.

context: but put ye on the Lord Jesus Christ, notice this phrase to end the chapter, make not provision for the flesh to fulfill the lusts thereof
point_summary: Putting on the Lord Jesus Christ means refusing to make provision for the flesh's desires—a call to active, deliberate holiness framed as urgent awakening before the sermon turns to John 13.

context: we talked about that truth in Romans 13, that we'd walk honestly,
point_summary: Walking honestly as commanded in Romans 13 applies not only to broader culture but equally—and urgently—to conduct within the church itself.

context: Notice in verse number nine of Romans 1
point_summary: Paul's unceasing, witnessed-before-God intercession for the Roman believers is cited as a biblical example of how the apostle prayed for others, paralleling his stated practice in Colossians 1:3.

context: First, I thank my God through Jesus Christ for you all, that your faith is spoken of throughout the whole world.
point_summary: Paul's unceasing intercessory prayer for the Roman believers serves as a biblical example of how Christians ought to pray persistently for one another.

context: For God is my witness, whom I serve with my spirit in the gospel of his Son, that without ceasing I make mention of you always in my prayers.
point_summary: Paul's unceasing, by-name intercession for the Roman believers ("without ceasing I make mention of you always in my prayers") serves as a biblical example of intercessory prayer for others.

context: we mentioned already that in Romans chapter number 1, he tells those believers that he's praying for them.
point_summary: Paul's intercessory prayer for the Roman believers in chapter 1 is later balanced by his urgent appeal in chapter 15 for those same believers to pray for him, illustrating the mutuality of intercession.

context: in fact, in verse number 30, notice what he says, now I beseech you.
point_summary: Paul's urgent appeal — "I beseech you… strive together with me in your prayers" — demonstrates that intercessory prayer is mutual, with even the apostle himself depending on the church's prayers on his behalf.

context: We see the use of the word in Romans chapter number 12, when he says, I beseech you therefore, brethren, that you, by the mercies of God, that you present your bodies a living sacrifice, wholly acceptable unto God, which is your reasonable service,
point_summary: Romans 12:1 is cited to illustrate Paul's use of the word "beseech" as urgent pleading, establishing the same intensity he brings in Romans 15:30 when asking believers to pray for him.

context: and be not conformed to this world, but be transformed by the renewing of your mind, that you may prove what is that good and acceptable and perfect will of God.
point_summary: The word "beseech" in Romans 12:2 is cited only to establish the urgency of Paul's pleading, illustrating that the same intensity he used to call believers to consecration he later directs toward requesting their intercessory prayer for himself.

context: now I beseech you, brethren, for the Lord Jesus Christ's sake and for the love of the Spirit, notice what he says, that ye strive together with me in your prayers to God for me.
point_summary: Paul's urgent appeal to the Roman believers — using the language of beseeching, meaning begging — was a specific, detailed request for intercessory prayer on his behalf, demonstrating that even apostles depend on the prayers of the church.

context: that I may be delivered from them that do not believe in Judea, and that my service which I have for Jerusalem may be accepted of the saints
point_summary: Paul's urgent appeal — using the language of beseeching — gives the Roman believers specific prayer requests for his protection and the acceptance of his ministry, illustrating that intercessory prayer can be concrete and directed.

context: that I may come unto you with joy by the will of God, and may with you be refreshed.
point_summary: Paul's urgent appeal in Romans 15:30–32 is presented as a model of specific, intercessory prayer requests, showing that even the Apostle Paul solicited the church's targeted prayer for his protection, ministry acceptance, and joyful arrival.

context: Now the God of peace be with you all, amen.
point_summary: Paul's urgent appeal in Romans 15:30–33 is cited to show that he specifically requested intercessory prayer from believers, naming concrete needs, as a pattern for mutual prayer within the body of Christ.

context: Romans chapter number 6, we are bound by sin.
point_summary: Before salvation, humanity is bound by sin — a condition Romans 6 is cited to establish as the spiritual reality underlying Hagar's allegorical representation of bondage under the old covenant.

context: Romans chapter number 8, I am so thankful for the truth of just the fact that there is no condemnation. It says this in Romans 8 and verse number 1, there is therefore now no condemnation to them which are in Christ Jesus, who walk not after the flesh but after the Spirit.
point_summary: No condemnation exists for those in Christ Jesus, but the sermon stresses the inverse: those outside Christ remain under the condemnation the old covenant's law exposes.

context: The Bible says that the wage of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord.
point_summary: Death as the wage of sin contrasts with the gift of eternal life through Christ, illustrating the new covenant's provision of grace, freedom, and life rather than bondage.

context: there is no condemnation for them that are in Christ Jesus
point_summary: Born again as children of promise, believers are no longer under condemnation but possess a new nature in Christ Jesus, free from the death and bondage of the old covenant.

context: and we know that all things work together for good to them that love God, to them who are the called according to his purpose.
point_summary: Romans 8:28 is cited as the familiar lead-in to verse 29, where the stated purpose of God's sovereign working is conformity to the image of Christ—identified here as the true goal of the Christian life.

context: for whom he did foreknow, he also did predestinate to what? To be conformed to the image of his son, that he might be the firstborn among many brethren.
point_summary: God's predestinating purpose, according to Romans 8:29, is not merely salvation but conformity to the image of Christ — which the sermon identifies as the very definition of sanctification.

context: you were the servants of sin, but now you're the servants of righteousness
point_summary: Romans 6:19's language of "fruits unto holiness" is identified as the same word as sanctification, grounding the expectation that salvation produces genuine transformation, not mere reformation, in the believer.

context: you were the servants of sin, but now you're the servants of righteousness
point_summary: Romans 6:22's language of "fruits unto holiness" is identified as the same word as sanctification, grounding the expectation that salvation produces genuine transformation, not mere reformation.

reference_text: Romans chapter 12 in verse number one
context: We're not to be conformed to this world. We see that in Romans chapter 12 in verse number one and two.
point_summary: Sanctification requires believers not to be conformed to this world but transformed by renewing the mind, aligning identity and citizenship with Christ rather than worldly patterns.

reference_text: Romans chapter 12 in verse number two
context: We're not to be conformed to this world. We see that in Romans chapter 12 in verse number one and two.
point_summary: Sanctification requires a transformed mind—not conformity to the world—as the renewing of one's mind reshapes a formerly contrary worldview into alignment with a biblical one.

context: the mercies of God that you present your body as a living sacrifice, holy, acceptable unto God, which is your reasonable service.
point_summary: Presenting the body as a living sacrifice and refusing conformity to the world's worldview is framed as the practical outworking of an identity rooted in Christ and citizenship in heaven.

context: And be not conformed to this world, but be ye transformed by the renewing of your mind.
point_summary: Non-conformity to the world and transformation through mind-renewal are presented as the essential shape of sanctification, contrasting the believer's heavenly citizenship with an incompatible worldly worldview.

context: Remember in the book of Romans, Paul says to them, how is it that you say don't steal, but you're stealing?
point_summary: Hypocrisy undermines the authority of one who calls others to a standard—Paul's challenge in Romans 2:21 demands that those who judge by God's Word must themselves live by it.

context: let God be true. And every man a liar.
point_summary: God's word, not human opinion or cultural consensus, is the sole standard of right and wrong — "let God be true and every man a liar."

context: There's none good. No, not one.
point_summary: No human being is basically good, for Scripture declares there is none righteous, not one — a truth the world resists but the Bible plainly affirms.

context: it really is following the pattern of Romans chapter number one.
point_summary: The cultural removal of God and the resulting collapse into self-authority follows the pattern Paul traces in Romans 1, where suppressing the knowledge of God leads to moral and societal disorder.

reference_text: the things that I would, I do not, the things that I would not, that I do
context: the things that I would, I do not, the things that I would not, that I do, that's the flesh and the spirit contrary one to the other
point_summary: The inner conflict Paul describes in Romans 7:19—wanting to do good yet doing evil—is presented as evidence of the indwelling Spirit, a struggle only the genuinely saved experience.

context: If you read Romans 1, you'll see it's the, I mean, it's just the devil has no new tricks, okay? That's just the situation we're dealing with today really is laid out for us in Romans 1, and we see that.
point_summary: Romans 1 is cited as a mirror of present social conditions, showing that the moral and spiritual disorder visible in contemporary society is not new but already mapped in Scripture.

context: Know ye not that to whom ye yield yourselves servants to obey, his servants ye are to whom ye obey, whether of sin unto death or of obedience unto righteousness.
point_summary: Yielding oneself to sin produces bondage to sin, while yielding to obedience produces righteousness—a reality that should shape how believers view the unsaved world around them.

context: But God be thanked that ye were the servants of sin.
point_summary: Obedience to the gospel transforms a person's bondage—from servant of sin to servant of righteousness—so that sinners sin because they are bound, not merely because they choose.

context: you being then made free from sin, ye became the servants of righteousness.
point_summary: Salvation transfers the believer from bondage to sin into the service of righteousness, freeing him from sin's power though not yet from its presence.

context: You notice it says in verse number 14, for sin shall not have dominion over you for you're not under the law but under grace.
point_summary: Grace's dominion over the believer means sin no longer holds sovereign power, delivering the saved from sin's penalty and breaking its binding power, though not yet its presence.

context: The wage of sin is death.
point_summary: Death as sin's wage (Rom 6:23) frames salvation as deliverance from sin's penalty—identified here as the second death of Revelation 20—while believers await final deliverance from sin's presence in heaven.

context: I am not ashamed of the Gospel of Christ. It was the power of God unto salvation
point_summary: The gospel's power unto salvation (Rom. 1:16) is invoked as the transforming force that moves a person from servant of sin to servant of righteousness through belief and obedience from the heart.

context: I am ready to preach the Gospel to you that are at Rome also
point_summary: Paul's readiness to preach the Gospel even in Rome illustrates that his understanding of Christ as Savior of the world compelled him to carry that message wherever he could go.

context: it was the Gospel that was the power of God unto salvation
point_summary: Paul's conversion served as a pattern—proof that the Gospel is the power of God unto salvation for any sinner who believes—motivating his readiness to preach it even in Rome.

context: Romans 10.13, I quoted a moment ago, but look back, if you will, to the book of Romans and look at chapter number 10.
point_summary: Paul's pattern of mercy toward the chief of sinners becomes the grounds for declaring Romans 10:13's promise of salvation to all who call, since even the zealously self-righteous can receive imputed righteousness through Christ.

context: In Romans chapter number 10, in verse number one, the Bible says, brethren, my heart's desire and prayer to God for Israel is that they might be saved.
point_summary: Paul's own pre-conversion zeal to establish his own righteousness—mirroring Israel's condition in Romans 10:1–4—illustrates why heartfelt religious sincerity without the imputed righteousness of Christ cannot save.

context: for they being ignorant of God's righteousness and going about to establish their own righteousness have not submitted themselves unto the righteousness of God.
point_summary: Ignorance of God's righteousness drives people to establish their own, rejecting the truth that salvation is accomplished entirely by Christ alone, not through any human cooperation.

context: For Christ is the end of the law for righteousness to every one that believeth.
point_summary: Christ being the end of the law for righteousness means salvation is entirely his accomplishment—not a cooperative effort between human works and divine grace, but Christ alone.

context: He says in verse number nine of Romans 10, that if thou shalt confess with thy mouth, the Lord Jesus, and shalt believe in thine heart that God hath raised him from the dead, thou shalt be saved.
point_summary: Salvation is entirely Christ's work alone — confession and belief in Romans 10:9 point to a "whosoever" gospel available to all sinners, not a cooperative human-divine effort.

context: For with the heart, man believeth unto righteousness and with the mouth, confession is made unto salvation.
point_summary: Salvation is entirely Christ's work alone—not a cooperative effort—so that "whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved" without distinction of person.

context: For the Scripture saith, whosoever believeth on him shall not be ashamed.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: For there is no difference between the Jew and the Greek for the same Lord over all is rich unto all that call upon him.
point_summary: The universal scope of salvation — "no difference between the Jew and the Greek" — grounds the "whosoever" of verse 13, showing that Christ's saving purpose extends to any sinner who calls upon Him.

context: For whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved.
point_summary: Salvation through Christ alone is universal in scope — "whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved" — because Christ came specifically to save sinners, establishing both the purpose and the pattern of redemption.

context: How then shall they call on him in whom they have not believed? And how shall they believe in him of whom they have not heard? And how shall they hear without a...
point_summary: Romans 10:14's chain of rhetorical questions grounds the necessity of preaching: sinners cannot call on Christ without hearing, and cannot hear without a preacher sent to proclaim Him.

context: For whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved.
point_summary: The universal promise of Romans 10:13 grounds the sermon's call to share the gospel, affirming that anyone who places faith in Christ's finished work will be saved.

context: Well, by being in the word of God. And we find, so then faith cometh by hearing and hearing by the word of God.
point_summary: Faith grows through engagement with the word of God, as Romans 10:17 is cited to explain how the Thessalonian church's exceedingly growing faith (2 Thess. 1:3) was produced.

context: Paul tells us in the book of Romans that we are to be conformed to the image of His dear Son, in Romans 8 and verse 29, speaking about God, speaking about Jesus, and we're to be conformed to His image.
point_summary: Conformity to Christ's image (Rom. 8:29) is cited as the scriptural basis for why believers must study the life of Christ rather than merely wearing WWJD slogans without knowing what Jesus would actually do.

context: For all have sinned and come short of the glory of God
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: How shall they hear without a preacher?
point_summary: Dying people cannot hear the gospel without someone sent to preach it, making personal evangelism an urgent, God-given responsibility every believer must take seriously.

context: And as we read Romans 5, the Bible says that, '...for as by one man sin entered into the world, and death by sin, and so death hath passed on all men, for that all have sinned.'
point_summary: Romans 5:12 is cited to establish the universal reach of Adam's sin — that death passed upon all men because all have sinned — as the necessary foundation for understanding one's lost condition before salvation.

context: Romans 3, '...for all have sinned and come short of the glory of God.'
point_summary: Romans 3:23 is cited to establish the universal guilt of all humanity before God as the necessary foundation for understanding one's need for salvation.

reference_text: the wages of sin is death
context: The Bible says the wages of sin is death.
point_summary: Hell's reality and its cause are grounded in Romans 6:23—sin's wages are spiritual death, meaning separation from God, which is the defining torment of that place.

context: the Apostle Paul in verse 14 of Romans chapter one, he says, I am debtor, both to the Greeks and to the barbarians, both to the wise and the unwise.
point_summary: Paul's sense of indebtedness in Romans 1:14 is grounded not in personal obligation but in being entrusted with a gospel meant not for himself alone but for all people.

context: I say the truth in Christ, I lie not, my conscience also bearing me witness in the Holy Ghost that I have great heaviness and continual sorrow in my heart.
point_summary: Paul's sworn testimony—"I say the truth in Christ, I lie not"—introduces his anguished, continual sorrow for unsaved Israel, illustrating the unashamed, burden-bearing heart that marks a faithful ambassador.

context: brethren, my heart's desire and prayer to God for Israel is that they might be saved.
point_summary: Paul's expressed heart's desire and prayer for Israel's salvation (Rom. 10:1) is used to illustrate that a faithful ambassador carries a genuine burden for the lost to be saved.

context: In Romans 15 verse 19, through mighty signs and wonders by the power of the Spirit of God so that from Jerusalem and round about unto Illyricum, I have fully preached the gospel of Christ
point_summary: Paul's burden-driven gospel proclamation—spanning Jerusalem to Illyricum through signs, wonders, and the Spirit's power—is cited as evidence that faithful ambassadors must carry a genuine burden for the lost, not merely obey a command.

context: We don't want to take the time to look at Romans 10 when Paul says, how shall they hear without a preacher?
point_summary: The rhetorical question "how shall they hear without a preacher?" is invoked as a scriptural plea underscoring the urgent need for faithful ambassadors willing to carry the gospel to the lost.

context: the gospel is the power of God unto salvation
point_summary: The gospel's power to save—drawn from Romans 1:16—is what made Paul's ministry in Thessalonica fruitful, and those converts became his glory and joy.

context: But how did they hear? They had to have a preacher.
point_summary: Ambassadors must go and preach because, as Paul's ministry in Thessalonica illustrates, people cannot hear the gospel and be saved without a human messenger sent to them.

reference_text: The wage of sin is death
context: The wage of sin is death. God loves you so much that He sent Jesus to die for you
point_summary: The wages of sin as death grounds the urgent call for the lost to be reconciled to God, with Christ's substitutionary death offered as the only remedy.

context: The Bible teaches us, if we could fast forward from Genesis 3 to Romans chapter number 5, and I want you to look there in Romans chapter 5, and the Bible teaches us about the validity of this record that God gives us in the book of Genesis.
point_summary: Romans 5:12 is cited to validate the Genesis 3 account, confirming that sin's entry into the world through one man is a historically attested fact corroborated across both Testaments.

context: And we find that in Romans chapter number 5, beginning in verse number 12, the Bible says this, wherefore, as by one man, sin entered into the world.
point_summary: Romans 5:12 is cited as New Testament confirmation of the Genesis 3 account, establishing that one man's sin brought death upon all humanity and that every person is therefore born a sinner in need of the transaction.

context: so death passed upon all men, for that all have sinned
point_summary: Romans 5:12 is cited as New Testament confirmation that Adam's sin introduced death to all humanity, establishing the universal sinfulness that made the redemptive transaction of 2 Corinthians 5:21 necessary.

context: for until the law, sin was not in the world, but sin is not imputed when there is no law
point_summary: Romans 5:13's reference to sin existing before the law—and death reigning from Adam to Moses—is used to confirm that universal sinfulness and death originated in Genesis 3, validating the Old Testament account and establishing why the redemptive transaction was necessary.

context: Nevertheless, death reigned from Adam to Moses, even over them that had not sinned after the similitude of Adam's transgression
point_summary: Death reigning from Adam to Moses—even over those without a law like Adam's—establishes that sin and its consequence are universal, making Christ's redemptive transaction the only sufficient remedy.

context: the things that I would, I do not, the things that I would not, that I do
point_summary: Paul's confession that he does what he would not and fails to do what he would illustrates the believer's ongoing struggle with indwelling sin, which is never eradicated at salvation.

context: who shall deliver me from the body of this death?
point_summary: Paul's cry in Romans 7:24 is cited to illustrate that even the believer remains in ongoing struggle with the flesh, which is not eradicated at salvation.

context: The Bible says this over in Romans chapter 4. Look there if you will.
point_summary: Righteousness credited apart from works—illustrated through Abraham—grounds the "great transaction" in which God gives his righteousness to those who have nothing to offer him.

context: Romans chapter 4, beginning in verse number 1. What shall we say then that Abraham our father's pertaining to the flesh hath found?
point_summary: Romans 4:1 introduces Abraham as the test case for whether justification comes by works or by grace, with the sermon pressing that no one—not even Abraham—can earn righteousness before God.

context: For if Abraham were justified by works, he hath whereof to glory, but not before God.
point_summary: Justification cannot be grounded in human works, for if it were, man could boast before God—but Scripture credits righteousness to Abraham through faith, not merit.

context: Abraham believed God and it was counted unto him for righteousness.
point_summary: Abraham's belief being "counted unto him for righteousness" is cited to establish that justification comes through faith credited as righteousness, not through works that God would owe as a debt.

context: Now to him that worketh is the reward not reckoned of grace, but of debt.
point_summary: Salvation cannot be earned as wages owed by God, because justification comes through faith counted as righteousness, not through works that would leave God indebted to man.

context: but to him that worketh not, but believeth on him that justifieth the ungodly, his faith is counted for righteousness.
point_summary: Justification belongs to the one who does not work for it but believes on God who justifies the ungodly—faith, not works, is what is counted for righteousness.

context: Even as David also described it, the blessedness of the man unto whom God imputeth righteousness without works.
point_summary: Imputed righteousness, as David's testimony in Romans 4:6 confirms, is granted by God apart from works—a blessedness defined by what God credits to the believer, not what the believer earns.

context: are they whose iniquities are forgiven and whose sins are covered.
point_summary: Blessed are those whose iniquities are forgiven and sins covered—Romans 4:7 is cited to establish that God imputes righteousness without works, leaving no room for merit-based salvation.

context: Blessed is the man to whom the Lord will not impute sin.
point_summary: Imputed righteousness is received without works — God credits righteousness to the believer's account while simultaneously not imputing sin, a double transaction David himself called "blessed."

context: God, He imputeth His righteousness on my account, on your account, if you're saved this morning, without works.
point_summary: Righteousness is imputed by God without works—David's testimony in Romans 4:6 confirms that God credits righteousness to the believer's account apart from any human merit.

context: Therefore, it is of faith that it might be by grace,
point_summary: Righteousness comes by faith so that it rests on grace alone, making the promise certain to all who share Abraham's faith, not only those under the law.

context: to the end that the promise might be sure to all the seed, not to that only which is of the law, but to that also which is of the faith of Abraham, who is the father of us all.
point_summary: Righteousness comes by faith and grace—not works—so that the promise of imputed righteousness is certain to all who, like Abraham, believe.

context: we read in Romans chapter number five in verse number eight, but God commendeth his love toward us and that while we were yet sinners, Christ died for us
point_summary: God's love is not merely declared but actively demonstrated—proven by Christ's death for sinners before any merit on humanity's part, as the word "commendeth" means to demonstrate.

context: the gospel is the power of God unto salvation
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: look at Romans chapter number four
point_summary: Romans 4 is introduced as the interpretive counterpart to James 2:21, demonstrating that apparent contradiction between justification by faith and justification by works dissolves under careful study of the specific events each passage references.

context: what shall we say then? Notice that Abraham, our father, as pertaining to the flesh, hath found
point_summary: Romans 4:1–2 is introduced as the counterpart to James 2:21 to show that Paul's use of Abraham addresses justification before God by faith, not works, ruling out any ground for boasting.

context: for if Abraham were justified by works, he hath whereof to glory, but not before God
point_summary: Romans 4:2 is read as Paul's argument that if Abraham's initial justification before God were by works, he would have grounds to boast—but grace through faith eliminates that boasting entirely.

context: for what saith the scripture?
point_summary: Abraham's justification by faith—not works—is established by appealing directly to Scripture as the sole authority, with the rhetorical question "what saith the scripture?" framing the verse as the decisive arbiter over tradition or theology.

context: Abraham believed God, and it was counted unto him for righteousness
point_summary: Paul's quotation of Genesis 15:6 in Romans 4:3 addresses a different event in Abraham's life than the works-based justification James describes in James 2:21, with "counted" and "imputed" treated as synonymous terms meaning credited to one's account.

context: not to him that worketh is the reward not reckoned of grace, but of debt
point_summary: Romans 4:4 is used to establish that salvation cannot be a wage owed to a worker but must be a gift, since God is debtor to no man.

context: for the wages of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord
point_summary: Salvation is entirely a gift of God, not a wage earned, because wages belong to debt while eternal life comes only through Jesus Christ by faith.

context: the whole chapter here in Romans chapter 4 deals with Abraham
point_summary: Romans 4 is cited to show that the imputation of righteousness to Abraham by faith—not works—establishes that salvation is always a gift, never a wage owed by God.

context: verse 23 of Romans 4. Now, it was not written for his sake alone that it was imputed to him
point_summary: The record of Abraham's imputed righteousness in Genesis 15 was written not for Abraham alone but for all who believe, establishing that God's righteousness is credited to faith in every age.

context: Notice verse 24, notice these words, but for us also. This is precious, this is important for us to understand, but for us also to whom it shall be imputed
point_summary: The record of Abraham's imputed righteousness in Genesis 15 was written not for Abraham alone but for all who believe on the risen Christ, to whom that same righteousness is imputed.

context: if we believe on him that raised up Jesus our Lord from the dead
point_summary: Abraham's recorded justification by faith was not written for his sake alone but for all who would believe on the risen Christ and receive imputed righteousness on the same terms.

context: as we read here in verse 24, if we believe on him that raised up Jesus our Lord from the dead
point_summary: Justification by faith in the risen Christ—who was delivered for our offenses and raised for our justification—is not merely Abraham's experience but a present certainty for all who believe, with righteousness shall be imputed.

context: who was delivered for our offenses, in verse 25, and was raised again for our justification
point_summary: Christ's resurrection is presented as the ground of justification, with Romans 4:25 read as Paul's declaration that Jesus was raised specifically "for our justification"—not merely for our offenses.

context: we notice at the end of Romans chapter 4 this wonderful declaration with regard to the truth of of salvation
point_summary: Romans 4's declaration that righteousness *shall* be imputed—not might or could be—grounds the certainty of salvation for all who believe, just as it was for Abraham.

context: if we believe the gospel is the power of God into salvation
point_summary: Belief that the gospel is the power of God unto salvation is demonstrated not by profession alone but by the active sharing of it with others.

context: Look at Romans chapter number eight, if you will
point_summary: Freedom from sin's bondage is grounded in Romans 8's declaration that the law of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus liberates humanity from the condemnation and dominion that sin holds over it.

context: there is therefore now no condemnation to them which are in Christ Jesus who walk not after the flesh, but after the spirit
point_summary: No condemnation exists for those in Christ Jesus, establishing the reality of sin's penalty — the law of sin and death — as the baseline condition of humanity from which freedom must come.

context: for the law of the spirit of life in Christ Jesus, notice this phrase, hath made me free from the law of sin and death
point_summary: Freedom from the law of sin and death—the universal human condition established in Romans 3, 5, and 6—is made possible through the law of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus.

context: all have sinned and come short of the glory of God
point_summary: All humanity stands under the law of sin and death, as Romans 3:23's declaration that all have sinned establishes the universal condition from which Christ's freedom in Romans 8:2 delivers.

context: We understand according to Romans chapter number five
point_summary: Sin's universal reign and its lethal consequence are grounded in Adam's transgression, establishing the "law of sin and death" from which Christ's Spirit alone can liberate humanity.

context: wherefore as by one man, sin entered into the world and death by sin. And so death passed upon all men for that all have sinned
point_summary: Sin's universal entry through one man and the resulting reign of death over all humanity establishes the "law of sin and death" from which Christ's redemptive work sets believers free.

context: for the wages of sin is death
point_summary: Sin's universal wages of death constitute an active, operational law governing all humanity—a condition from which, the sermon argues, only Christ can liberate.

context: the law of sin and death
point_summary: The law of sin and death is a still-operational, universal law from which Christ alone liberates, contrasted against humanity's default condition of bondage under sin's penalty.

context: how beautiful are the feet of them that preach the gospel, Romans chapter number 10
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: There in Romans chapter number eight, we think about the condition of humanity and we see here about the truth of the law of sin and death
point_summary: The law of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus, set against the law of sin and death, is presented as the liberating reality made possible by Christ's compassion.

context: for the law of the spirit of life in Christ Jesus
point_summary: The law of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus is presented as the compassionate divine reality that accomplishes what the law of sin and death could not — bringing freedom through what Christ did.

context: for what the law could not do
point_summary: Where the law could not bring freedom—being weakened by the flesh—God's compassion accomplished it by sending His own Son to condemn sin in the flesh, liberating humanity from the law of sin and death.

context: for what the law could not do. It was impossible in that it was weak through the flesh. God sending his own son in the likeness of sinful flesh for out and for sin. Notice what the Bible says, condemned sin in the flesh.
point_summary: Where the law could not bring life—being weakened by the flesh—God's sending of His own Son accomplished what the law never could: condemning sin in the flesh and freeing believers from the law of sin and death.

context: but God commendeth his love toward us, and that while we were yet sinners, what's it say? Christ died for us
point_summary: God's love is demonstrated in Christ dying for sinners as the one sufficient substitute whose shed blood paid the full cost of freedom from sin's bondage.

context: one in the book of Acts, one in the book of Romans
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: For the gospel is the power of God unto salvation
point_summary: The gospel's power to save remains undiminished by rejection, and that unchanging reality is the ground for continued, unashamed proclamation even when response is mixed.

context: look in Romans chapter number 10
point_summary: True freedom is not political or national but spiritual, as Romans 10 is introduced to show that America's greatest bondage is bondage to sin, not to any earthly power.

context: those in Romans 8, or excuse me, in John 8
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: In Romans chapter number 10, the Bible says, beginning in verse number nine, notice here, it says this, that if thou, that's singular, that if thou shalt confess
point_summary: Salvation from spiritual bondage is a personal, individual decision—each person must confess Christ and believe in the resurrection to be saved.

context: for with the heart, man believeth unto righteousness and with the mouth, confession is made unto salvation
point_summary: Salvation is a personal, individual decision — heart belief unto righteousness and mouth confession unto salvation — not a collective act, contrasting the singular "thou" of Romans 10 with the plural "ye" of John 3.

context: whosoever believeth on him shall not be ashamed
point_summary: Salvation through faith in Christ is a universal, personal offer — "whosoever believeth" — with no distinction between Jew or Greek, underscoring that the individual choice to believe carries eternal consequence.

context: For there's no difference between the Jew and the Greek. For the same Lord over all is rich and all that call upon him
point_summary: No distinction exists between Jew and Greek before God, who is equally Lord over all and rich in mercy toward every person who calls upon Him.

context: for whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved
point_summary: Salvation through calling on the Lord is universally available—no distinction between persons—making it an individual choice each person must personally make.

context: in Romans chapter number eight, that there's this law that's existing, and it's the law of sin and death
point_summary: Romans 8's "law of sin and death" is invoked as the bondage from which Christ offers freedom, making rejection of that freedom a choice with the consequence of remaining under condemnation.

context: every knee is gonna bow and every tongue is gonna confess
point_summary: Every knee bowing and every tongue confessing is presented as an inevitable, universal reality—making the timing of that confession, before or after death, the decisive consequence of one's choice.

context: if there had not been a law that said thou shalt not covet, I wouldn't have known that I was coveting
point_summary: The law's function is not to save but to expose sin — as Paul illustrates in Romans, the commandment against coveting revealed that he was a coveter.

context: Romans chapter number six talks about it quite a bit and talking about the fact that we have been made free
point_summary: Romans 6's declaration that sin shall not have dominion over believers under grace is cited to clarify that Christian freedom is freedom from sin's rule, not license to live however one pleases.

context: for sin shall not have dominion over you
point_summary: Romans 6's declaration that sin shall not have dominion over believers grounds the sermon's distinction between true Christian freedom—freedom to serve Christ—and the false notion that grace permits living however one pleases.

context: they would confess with their mouth and believe in their heart
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: God is no respecter of persons
point_summary: God's impartiality—declared in Romans 2:11—is presented as the biblical foundation that expels partiality when believers allow their faith to shape their behavior.

context: Romans chapter 10, we're so familiar with verse 13
point_summary: The universal scope of salvation in Romans 10:13 is grounded in verse 12's declaration that there is no difference between Jew and Greek, since the same Lord is rich unto all who call.

context: for whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved
point_summary: Romans 10:12's context around verse 13 grounds the sermon's point that God shows no partiality in salvation, because "there is no difference between the Jew and the Greek" and the gospel has no wrong address.

context: for there is no difference between the Jew and the Greek for the same Lord over all is rich unto all that call upon him
point_summary: God's impartiality in salvation is confirmed in Romans 10:12—there is no distinction between Jew and Greek, making the gospel universally addressed and partiality among believers inexcusable.

context: Look in Romans chapter six, if you will. It's a verse of scripture that many of us could probably quote. this morning and we find in Romans chapter 6 in verse number 23
point_summary: Romans 6:23 is introduced as a foundational gospel-sharing verse, with the sermon pausing on its first clause to emphasize that death is the earned wage humanity receives for sin.

context: you'll find yourself here in Romans 6 in verse number 23. The Bible says that the wages of sin is death
point_summary: Salvation is contrasted with earned wages: sin's wage is death, but eternal life is a gift received entirely apart from human contribution, since any personal contribution would disqualify it as a gift.

context: but there's a great contrast in Romans 6 in verse 23 for the wage of sin is death but the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord
point_summary: Salvation is distinguished from works by contrasting the wage of sin (earned death) with the gift of eternal life, which ceases to be a gift if even one percent is contributed by the recipient.

context: look at Romans chapter 5 God commended his love toward us and that while we're yet sinners Christ died for us
point_summary: God's love and saving initiative are grounded in Romans 5:8—Christ died for sinners while they were still enemies, making salvation entirely God's act, not something earned by human effort or good works.

context: Let God be true, and every man a liar
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: We are to be conformed to the image of his dear son, according to Romans 8 and verse 29
point_summary: Conformity to Christ's image is cited as God's overarching plan for believers, grounding the call to visible, behavioral evidence of faith.

context: those things that were written aforetime were written for our admonition, they were written for our instruction
point_summary: Paul's deep familiarity with Old Testament prayer records—written for our instruction—shaped his own committed prayer life, illustrating that Scripture's preserved examples exist to teach and admonish believers.

context: Notice what he says in Romans chapter number one, in verse number nine, it says this, for God is my witness, whom I serve with my spirit in the gospel of his Son. Notice what he says, that without ceasing, I make mention of you always in my prayers.
point_summary: Paul's oath-like appeal — "God is my witness" — is used to establish that his unceasing, consistent intercession for the Roman believers was a verifiable, habitual reality, not an occasional gesture.

context: He says in Romans one that God is my witness. I'm praying for you always without ceasing. From the day that I heard of it.
point_summary: Paul's invocation of God as witness in Romans 1:9 is cited to demonstrate that a life marked by prayer requires not only commitment but consistent, unceasing intercession for others.

context: if God before us who can be against us
point_summary: God being "on my side" (Ps. 118:6) is grounded in the truth of Romans 8:31—that if God is for us, no human opposition can ultimately prevail.

context: We are more than conquerors through Christ
point_summary: Confidence against all opposition rests on the assurance that if God is for us and we are on His side, we are more than conquerors through Christ.

context: and we know that all things work together for good
point_summary: Romans 8:28's opening words "and we know" establish that the believer's confidence in all things working together for good is a settled certainty, not a partial or conditional promise.

context: all things work together for good to them that love God to them who are the called according to his purpose
point_summary: God's promise that all things — without exception — work together for good is grounded in the confidence of "and we know," and extends through the golden chain of predestination, calling, justification, and glorification to an inseparable love in Christ.

context: For whom he did for know he also did predestinate to be conformed to the image of his son
point_summary: God's predestinating purpose—conforming believers to the image of his Son—is the "good" toward which all things work, grounding the confidence that nothing can separate the suffering believer from Christ's love.

context: Whom he did predestinate them. He also called and whom he called them He also justified and whom he justified them. He also glorified
point_summary: God's golden chain of salvation in Romans 8:30—predestination, calling, justification, and glorification—grounds the believer's confidence that all things, without exception, work together for good.

context: What shall we say to these things? What shall we then say to these things if God before us? Who can be against us?
point_summary: God's unassailable position "for us" — demonstrated by giving his own Son — means no adversary, circumstance, or suffering can ultimately stand against the believer.

context: How shall he not with him also freely give us all things who shall lay anything to the charge of God's elect?
point_summary: God's willingness to give His own Son becomes the ground for confidence that He will freely give believers all things, leaving nothing outside His providential care.

context: It is God that justified
point_summary: God's justifying work on behalf of the elect means no charge brought against them can stand, grounding the believer's security in God's own verdict rather than circumstance.

context: Who is he that condemneth? It is Christ that died yea rather that is risen again Who is even at the right hand of God who also make an intercession for us?
point_summary: Christ's intercession at the right hand of God is cited within the Romans 8 chain of assurance — predestination through glorification — establishing that nothing, including suffering, can separate believers from God's love.

context: who shall separate us from the love of Christ shall tribulation or distress or persecution or famine or nakedness or peril or sword
point_summary: Romans 8:35's list of hardships — tribulation, distress, persecution, famine, nakedness, peril, sword — is drawn as a parallel to 2 Corinthians 12:10, showing that such sufferings cannot separate believers from Christ's love.

context: shall these things separate us from the love of Christ
point_summary: No circumstance, power, or created thing can sever the believer from God's love in Christ Jesus — a conviction Paul frames as absolute persuasion, with no wiggle room left.

context: For thy sake we are killed all the day long. We are accounted as sheep for the slaughter.
point_summary: Paul's citation of Romans 8:36 — suffering as sheep for the slaughter — is immediately answered by verse 37's declaration that believers are more than conquerors, inseparable from God's love.

context: Nay in all these things We are more than conquerors through him that loved us
point_summary: Suffering, tribulation, and hardship cannot sever the believer from God's love, making the Christian not merely a survivor but more than a conqueror through Christ.

context: neither death nor life nor angels or principalities nor powers nor things present nor things to come
point_summary: Paul's absolute persuasion that no created thing can sever believers from God's love grounds the sermon's call to embrace trials as the arena of sufficient grace rather than evidence of abandonment.

context: nor height nor depth nor any other creature Shall be able to separate us from the love of God, which is in Christ Jesus our Lord
point_summary: No created thing — not death, life, angels, powers, height, nor depth — can sever the believer from God's love in Christ Jesus, a certainty the sermon presents as absolute, unconditional assurance amid trials.

context: the gospel is the power of God unto salvation
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: professing themselves to be wise, they became fools
point_summary: Claiming wisdom apart from Scripture leads to foolishness, as God's word proves superior to all human learning and corrects academia rather than being corrected by it.

context: Romans chapter 1 in verse 22, professing themselves to be wise, they became fools
point_summary: Professing worldly wisdom apart from God results in folly, contrasted here with the divine wisdom from above that James calls believers to seek.

context: The wages of sin is death. The gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ, our Lord.
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Awake, what we read in the book of Romans, right?
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: You can think about Romans five and eight and various portions of scripture
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: there's now therefore no condemnation to those that are in Christ Jesus
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

reference_text: For the wages of sin is death
context: For the wages of sin is death
point_summary: Sin's penalty is death — a debt demanding justice, not a blind eye — which Christ paid in full through his substitutionary death, enabling true remission.

context: sin entered into the world and death by sin. We read that in Romans chapter number five
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: the wages of sin is death
point_summary: Death's universal wage over sinful humanity establishes the need for the payment Christ made, framing "it is finished" as the sin debt of all mankind paid in full.

context: for whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved
point_summary: Romans 10:13 is cited to establish that while Christ's atoning blood provides pardon for all humanity, each individual must personally call upon the Lord to receive that salvation.

context: whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved
point_summary: Salvation is received as a gift, not earned—illustrated by the preacher's personal testimony of calling on the Lord and being saved, citing Romans 10:13 as the basis.

context: by one man, sin entered into the world
point_summary: Sin's entry into the world through one man (Romans 5:12) is traced directly back to Genesis 3 as the backdrop establishing humanity's need for the promised Messiah.

context: all have sinned and come short of the glory of God
point_summary: Sin's universality—every human being born with a sin nature, falling short of God's glory—establishes the need from which Christ came to save his people.

context: the wages of sin is death
point_summary: Sin's universal condition carries a universal cost — the wages of sin is death — establishing why Christ's birth and saving mission were necessary for every human being.

context: if the wages of sin is death
point_summary: Sin's universal separating power is the problem Christ's birth addresses—because the wages of sin is death, every person, regardless of the degree of their sin, stands in need of a Savior.

context: All men are liars
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: there is therefore now no condemnation to them which are in Christ Jesus, who walk not after the flesh, but after the Spirit
point_summary: Redemption through Christ's blood liberates sinners from the law of sin and death, so that those once condemned under that law now stand in Christ with no condemnation.

context: for the law of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus, notice, hath made me free from the law of sin and death
point_summary: The law of the Spirit of life in Christ Jesus liberates sinners from the law of sin and death, making redemption from condemnation possible for those who are in Christ Jesus.

context: Paul says, how would I know that I'm a sinner unless the law said, thou shalt not covet?
point_summary: The law's function is to expose sin and condemn the sinner, as illustrated by Paul's statement that he would not have known covetousness as sin apart from the law's prohibition.

context: the Bible says that the wages of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ, our Lord
point_summary: Sin's wages are death, but eternal life is God's gift through Christ — a contrast the sermon uses to press the question of personal trust in Jesus as Savior.

context: whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved
point_summary: The universal offer of salvation — "whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved" — is presented as the greatest gift, surpassing any earthly Christmas present, available to the lost that very morning.

context: therefore being justified by faith, we have peace with God through our Lord Jesus Christ
point_summary: Justification by faith through Christ is the sole basis for peace with God, and this peace grounds the church's responsibility to preach the gospel to all people.

context: brethren my heart's desire and prayer to God for Israel is that they might be saved
point_summary: Paul's distinct burden for ethnic Israel's salvation (Rom. 10:1) is cited as hermeneutical evidence against replacement theology, demonstrating that Israel and the church remain separate entities in God's prophetic program.

context: I have great heaviness and continual sorrow in my heart why for I could wish that myself were accursed from Christ for my brethren my kinsmen according to the flesh
point_summary: Paul's anguish over his Jewish kinsmen in Romans 9:2 is cited as evidence that Israel remains a distinct people from the church, undermining replacement theology.

context: chapter 9 and 10 of the book of Romans
point_summary: Paul's anguish over unsaved Israel in Romans 9 is cited as evidence that the church cannot be equated with Israel, since replacement theology would render his sorrow self-contradictory.

context: We finished up last week looking at the book of Romans in chapter 11 and studied through all of that
point_summary: Romans 11's teaching on Israel's partial blindness—resulting from the nation's rejection of Messiah—establishes why the Jewish people will be vulnerable to receiving the Antichrist as a counterfeit messiah.

context: the Bible says that the wage of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ, our Lord
point_summary: The wages of sin is death, but eternal life is a gift paid for by Christ's blood—a gift that must be personally received or rejected.

context: His spirit bears witness with our spirit
point_summary: The Holy Spirit's indwelling witness with our spirit is cited as the New Testament believer's assurance that the Comforter permanently resides within them, unlike the Spirit's temporary presence in Old Testament individuals.

context: the wages of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ our Lord
point_summary: Eternal life is framed as God's unearned gift contrasted with sin's earned wage of death, cited as a gospel appeal to the unsaved.

context: Paul asked the question in Romans chapter number eight, what can separate us from the love of God?
point_summary: Romans 8's rhetorical question and its answer — that nothing can separate believers from God's love — is invoked to assure those enduring trials that God's love remains constant, fueling their love for him in return.

context: For we know. For we know. Romans 8 verse 28. Sometimes we just start with the all things work together for good to them that love God
point_summary: The certainty embedded in "for we know" grounds the believer's confidence that God is purposefully working all things—including trials—for good, making endurance possible rather than optional.

context: all things do work together for good to them that love God, to them who are the called according to His purpose
point_summary: Enduring trials in confident faith rests on the assurance of "for we know" — that God purposefully works all things together for good to those called according to His purpose.

reference_text: For whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved
context: For whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved
point_summary: The universal scope of Christ's redemption — purchasing people from every kindred, tongue, and nation — demands worldwide gospel proclamation, since "whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved" only if someone first goes and tells them.

reference_text: how shall they believe on him in whom they've not heard and how shall they hear without a preacher
context: how shall they believe on him in whom they've not heard and how shall they hear without a preacher
point_summary: Redemption purchased by Christ's blood reaches every kindred, tongue, and nation only because preachers are sent—making global missions the necessary link between the cross and the redeemed multitude of Revelation 5.

context: for whosoever shall call upon the name of the Lord shall be saved
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: Faith cometh by hearing and hearing by the word of God
point_summary: Faith comes by hearing the word of God, cited here to underscore why Bible access and distribution matter for those who have never encountered Scripture.

context: be not conformed to this world, but be ye transformed by the renewing of your mind
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: we read in Romans 6 and verse 14. And it's this, that sin shall not have dominion over you
point_summary: Believers hold the precious promise that sin shall not have dominion over them, yet they still possess the old nature and must actively reckon with ongoing struggle against the flesh.

context: For the wages of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ, our Lord
point_summary: Salvation delivers believers from sin's penalty — "the wages of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life" — yet the saved still contend with the old nature and must actively put it off.

context: the wages of sin is death
point_summary: The unsaved person's sin debt—"the wages of sin is death"—condemns them to the lake of fire, making salvation the one sin they must address above all else.

context: for all have sinned and come short of the glory of God
point_summary: Universal sinfulness establishes the lost person's condemnation and unpaid sin debt, which only Christ's imputed righteousness at salvation can resolve.

context: I quoted already from Romans 6 and verse number 14, that sin shall not have dominion over you
point_summary: Sin's dominion over believers is broken by God's promise, and that promise in Romans 6:14 is to be actively claimed as part of a deliberate plan for walking in victory.

context: I know that in me, that is my flesh dwelleth no good thing
point_summary: Fleshly inability to produce good—echoing Romans 7:18's "in me dwelleth no good thing"—is cited to ground the believer's victory entirely in God's working, not personal effort.

context: sin shall not have dominion over us
point_summary: Sin's dominion over believers is broken, making present victory possible and unbroken fellowship with God an attainable reality worth protecting at all cost.

context: I am not ashamed of the gospel of Christ, for it, the gospel, is the power of God unto salvation to everyone that believeth, to the Jew first and also to the Greek
point_summary: Romans 1:16 is cited to establish that the gospel is not merely good news but the very power of God unto salvation, grounding Paul's declaration in 1 Corinthians 15 within that same unashamed proclamation.

context: It is the power of God unto salvation
point_summary: The gospel is identified as the exclusive power of God unto salvation—not grace plus works, but Christ alone—grounding the church's mandate to preach it to every creature.

context: Sin, the wage of sin is death. But the gift of God is eternal life through Jesus Christ, our Lord.
point_summary: Sin's penalty — death — is contrasted with the free gift of eternal life through Christ, underscoring that salvation cannot be earned but only accepted by faith in what Christ finished on the cross.

context: look at the book of Romans. We'll look there first. And in Romans chapter number 11
point_summary: Israel's national rejection of Jesus as Messiah did not nullify God's purposes for the Jewish people, as Romans 11 is introduced to show a "very important truth" about Israel's ongoing place in God's plan.

context: in verse 25, we don't have time to read all of Romans 11
point_summary: The mystery Paul reveals in Romans 11:25—previously unknown and now progressively disclosed—is cited as foundational to understanding the tribulation's purpose: preparing Israel to receive her true Messiah.

context: look at verse 20. It says, well, because of unbelief, they were broken off and thou standest by faith. Be not high-minded, but fear. For if God spared not the natural branches, take heed lest he also spare not thee.
point_summary: Israel's broken-off status due to unbelief, contrasted with the Gentile church standing by faith, frames the tribulation's purpose as preparing Israel to finally receive her Messiah.

context: professing themselves to be wise, they became fools
point_summary: Brief mention; no exposition in this segment.

context: my heart's desire and prayer to God for Israel is that they might be saved
point_summary: Israel's spiritual blindness is described as partial, not total, as evidenced by Paul himself—a Jewish man—expressing heartfelt prayer for his people's salvation in Romans 10:1.

context: this blindness in part happened to Israel, but then notice the last phrase, until the fullness of the Gentiles be come in
point_summary: Israel's partial blindness is presented as a divinely ordained, temporary condition lasting until the fullness of the Gentiles comes in, while still permitting individual Jewish people to be saved in the present age.

context: all Israel shall be saved. As it is written, there shall come out of Zion, the deliverer and shall turn away ungodliness from Jacob
point_summary: Israel's partial, temporary blindness will culminate in national salvation when the fullness of the Gentiles comes in, confirming that God's covenant promises to rebellious Jacob remain irrevocable.

context: for this is my covenant unto them. When I shall take away
point_summary: Israel's future covenant salvation—the removal of their sins—is grounded not in their present faithfulness but in God's irrevocable promises, even amid their ongoing rebellion and rejection of Messiah.

context: their sins as concerning the gospel, they are enemies for your sakes, but as touching the election, they are beloved for the father's sakes
point_summary: Despite Israel's present enmity toward the gospel, Romans 11:28 is cited to affirm that they remain beloved and elect, and that God's covenant promises to them are irrevocable and will yet be fulfilled.

context: for the gifts and calling of God are without repentance
point_summary: God's irrevocable gifts and calling toward Israel ground the assurance that, despite her present rebellion and rejection, she remains beloved and will yet be saved.

context: for as ye in times past have not believed God, yet they now obtain mercy through their unbelief
point_summary: Israel's present unbelief is shown to be the very instrument through which Gentiles received mercy, establishing a reciprocal pattern that will ultimately result in Israel's own salvation.

context: Even so, have these also now not believed that through your mercy, they also may obtain mercy
point_summary: Israel's future mercy is conditioned on faith, not ethnicity—the partial blindness and unbelief of Romans 11 will give way to salvation only through the covenant promise to take away their sins.

context: for God hath concluded them all in unbelief, that he might have mercy upon all
point_summary: God's concluding all in unbelief so that He might have mercy on all establishes the theological ground for Israel's future salvation, showing that divine mercy—not merit—is the basis.

context: all Israel shall be saved
point_summary: All Israel being saved (Rom. 11:26) does not mean Jewish people are automatically saved, but refers to a future national salvation after God purges and brings Israel into covenant through judgment.

context: verse 25 and 26. He says that blindness in part has happened to Israel until the fullness of the Gentiles be come in
point_summary: Israel's partial and temporary blindness, according to this sermon, will last only until the complete number of Gentiles is saved, at which point the church age ends and God's focus returns to Israel.

context: verse 25 and 26. He says that blindness in part has happened to Israel until the fullness of the Gentiles be come in
point_summary: Israel's national salvation in Romans 11:26 is tied to the Tribulation period, when God's redemptive focus shifts back to Israel through Jewish witnesses preparing the nation to receive her Messiah.

context: in verse 26, and so all Israel shall be saved as it is written, there shall come out of Zion the deliverer and shall turn away ungodliness from Jacob
point_summary: Israel's blindness being partial and temporary will lift when the fullness of the Gentiles is complete, after which the entire seven-year tribulation serves to prepare Israel to receive her Messiah at his return.

context: and so all Israel shall be saved
point_summary: Israel's national salvation at the end of the tribulation—not ethnic privilege—is the point of Romans 11:26, as those surviving Jews who acknowledge Jesus as Messiah enter the millennial reign in their flesh.

context: all have sinned and come short of the glory of God
point_summary: Universal sinfulness applies to Jewish people as much as Gentiles, refuting the false teaching that Jewish ethnicity alone secures salvation.

context: what advantage then hath the Jew? Or what profit is there in circumcision?
point_summary: Israel's possession of the oracles of God (Rom. 3:1–2) is cited to show that Jewish heritage confers privilege, not automatic salvation, since all have sinned and must personally receive Messiah.

reference_text: Chapter three, verse one and two
context: unto them were committed the oracles of God
point_summary: Israel's possession of the oracles of God (Rom. 3:2) is cited to show that Jewish privilege was real but did not guarantee salvation, since Israel ultimately rejected what was entrusted to them.

context: What can separate us from the love of God?
point_summary: Against the enemy's attempts to make believers doubt God's love during trials, Romans 8:35 is invoked to affirm that nothing can separate the believer from that love—a fact to hold onto over feelings.

context: All things work together for good
point_summary: In trials, the anchor of Romans 8:28 is not feeling but knowing — the verse's opening words "and we know" ground the believer's confidence in revealed fact rather than emotion.

context: all things work together for good
point_summary: God's promise in Romans 8:28 rests on the certainty of "and we know," a grounding in fact rather than feeling that all things—without exception—work together for good.

context: and we know that all things work together for good. To them that love God, to them who are the called according to his purpose
point_summary: God's sovereign orchestration of "all things" — not some or most — for the good of those who love him surpasses human comprehension, just as a cook combines ingredients whose purpose the observer cannot yet see.

context: he said that he was gonna work all things together for good. To them who love God, to them who are the called according to his purpose
point_summary: God's sovereign purpose in trials—that he works all things together for good for those who love him—grounds the call to expect God to work rather than succumb to despair.

context: Paul said this in Romans 12 and verse 18. And I think it's a verse that's important that we apply properly. He says, if it be possible, as much as lieth in you live peaceably with all men.
point_summary: The personal responsibility embedded in "as much as lieth in you" means believers cannot easily excuse unresolved conflict without first examining whether they have genuinely exhausted their own effort toward peace.

context: nothing, Paul says in the book of Romans, can separate us from the love of God, which is in Christ Jesus
point_summary: Nothing in all creation can separate believers from the love of God in Christ Jesus, cited here to underscore the unbreakable nature of the divine love expressed at Calvary.

context: We often also quote Romans 5, verse number eight, God commendeth his love toward us, and that while we're yet sinners, Christ died for us
